page_content: Homage to Him, the Blessed One, the Worthy One,

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page v

page_content: the Perfectly Self-Enlightened One

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page vi

page_content: You have to play,

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page vi

page_content: play with awareness

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page vi

page_content: and the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page vi

page_content: Then it becomes very interesting.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page vi

page_content: Use your know-how and wit.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page vi

page_content: If meditation feels like a responsibility,

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page vi

page_content: it’ll just be a burden.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page vi

page_content: Create your world.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page vi

page_content: It’s a game called Master Mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Acknowledgements, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page vii

page_content: My special gratitude goes to my teacher, the late Venerable Shwe Oo Min Sayadaw Bhaddanta Kosalla Mahāthera, who taught me the Dhamma and right attitude for my spiritual development and meditation practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Acknowledgements, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page vii

page_content: I want to express my appreciation to all yogis.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Acknowledgements, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page vii

page_content: Their questions and difficulties have once again inspired many of the explanations in this book.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Acknowledgements, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page vii

page_content: I really hope that this book will help yogis better understand mindfulness meditation and deepen their practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Acknowledgements, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page vii

page_content: Finally, I would like to thank everyone who has contributed to the completion of this book.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Acknowledgements, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page vii

page_content: Sayadaw U Tejaniya

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page ix

page_content: It doesn’t take long for a yogi (meditator) new to Sayadaw U Tejaniya to notice humor permeating his teachings.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page ix

page_content: Whether it is making an observation on his surroundings or commenting on the environment of the mind, Sayadaw, or “the Teacher” as his disciples fondly call him, delivers his teachings with a good dose of wit.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page ix

page_content: While the delivery may come with a bit of levity, Sayadaw is quite serious about the subject of Dhamma.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page ix

page_content: Using his own experiences as guide and gradually honing his presentation, Sayadaw encourages meditators to always be persistent, experiment, and dig deeper into the Dhamma.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page ix

page_content: He relates to his lay students through pithy business metaphors and regularly describes his own experiences in learning to meditate in the midst of managing the family business and before ordaining permanently in 1996.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page ix

page_content: Sayadaw is a strong proponent of applying the Buddha’s teachings to daily activities in a monastery, at home, and at work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page ix

page_content: When his long- time disciples make the migration to the Shwe Oo Min Dhamma Sukha Tawya Meditation Center in Yangon, Myanmar, one of his first questions is typically about their practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page x

page_content: Some may reply in detail while others sheepishly answer that they have returned to jumpstart their flagging practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page x

page_content: Sayadaw often encourages yogis to continue cultivating their own energy and wisdom after a retreat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page x

page_content: To help post-retreat, some of Sayadaw’s retreats even have participants going mindfully about their business during the day in their own homes or workplaces and discussing their eperiences and discoveries in evening and weekend Q&A sessions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page x

page_content: Other retreats have incorporated mindful talking periods during the day.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page x

page_content: Meditating in daily life means building up these skills with diligence and right inquiry.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page x

page_content: It means asking the right questions at the right time to spur on an internal curiosity to investigate further.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page x

page_content: The answers will reveal themselves to appropriate questions so long as we apply right view and perseverance to our meditation, Sayadaw reassures us.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page x

page_content: When meditation gathers momentum, awareness becomes more natural.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page x

page_content: There is less doing and more recognizing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page x

page_content: This book was compiled as a continuing thread to the question of what net?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page x

page_content: We each have our own versions of the first few days of retreat ecitement calming down to a steady mind, building momentum over a week or two, only to see it crumble in the anticipation of reentering daily life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page xi

page_content: Forgetfulness sets in as we leave Sayadaw’s presence and the supportive environment of the center.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page xi

page_content: Even in the occasional outing to the village or city while at the Shwe Oo Min Meditation Center, yogis often report to Sayadaw that there are just so many objects out there!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page xi

page_content: To which Sayadaw often replies that there are only six sense doors and knowing is of only the six senses at the six sense doors inside and out of retreat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page xi

page_content: Sayadaw noted in a recent retreat in California that most of us meet a limited set of people in our daily lives and go about our lives with a limited set of people, pointing out that if we observed our reactions to others with right view, we could learn to resolve these challenges ourselves.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 6 on page xi

page_content: Mostly we don’t watch and often not with right view.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page xi

page_content: It is our hope that Sayadaw’s words will inspire all of us to continue wholeheartedly, being as sincere and serious as Sayadaw is about being aware and cultivating awareness at home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page xi

page_content: In compiling these pages, we asked ourselves: What are the most salient issues to practicing in daily life?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page xi

page_content: What might arise more off the cushion that we may not experience as much on retreat?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page xi

page_content: What stories from Sayadaw’s own experiences can address the challenges of daily life practice?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page xi

page_content: To get at some of these answers, we have tracked down and edited transcriptions from the more recent retreats abroad.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page xii

page_content: While Sayadaw teaches through guided meditations and Q&As led by yogi questions, we have compiled this book thematically so that a reader can use it in daily life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page xii

page_content: What do we do when the retreat is over?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page xii

page_content: “Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change” begins the book while an abbreviated “Mindfulness in Brief” caps the book in the Appendix.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page xii

page_content: The beginning chapters address the triumvirate of greed, aversion, and delusion while latter chapters delve into more specific topics for daily life, which is anytime, anywhere off the cushion although we could also be practicing on the cushion in our daily lives as well!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page xii

page_content: While it may seem at odds to have sections on cultivating wholesome minds, inquiry, and non-doing all in the same book, each of these pieces answers different needs at different times in our meditation practice, whether within a span of minutes or months.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page xii

page_content: It feels like we have been editing for quite some time, and we have, over some years, having worked on this manuscript in the midst of daily life, and understand fully when life just takes over.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page xii

page_content: We have tried to stick to our own page limits for this daily-life book, lest we continue to edit forever!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page xii

page_content: If this is the first book you are picking up with Sayadaw U Tejaniya’s teachings, may we suggest the illustrated Don’t Look Down on the Defilements for a funny and quick primer on the right attitude for meditation, Dhamma Everywhere: Welcoming Each Moment with Awareness + Wisdom for a more in-depth look, and Awareness Alone is Not Enough with Q&A’s from different retreats?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page xiii

page_content: These are all given out freely and can be accessed online at www.ashintejaniya.org or through Wisdom Streams, www.wisdomstreams.org in North America.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page xiii

page_content: For more auditory learners, please check for Sayadaw’s retreat audio on www.dharmaseed.org.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 10, sentence number 4 on page xiii

page_content: When Awareness Becomes Natural: A Guide to Cultivating Mindfulness in Everyday Life is a commercial publication that can be accessed through Shambhala and other sources.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 10, sentence number 5 on page xiii

page_content: We appreciate greatly your very vital contributions towards future free publications globally; please reach out to any of us at info@wisdomstreams.org.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page xiii

page_content: When we welcome the Dhamma into our daily lives we reap the benefits of the Buddha’s teachings.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page xiii

page_content: Our deepest gratitude goes to Sayadaw for patiently lighting this path of awareness + wisdom, and teaching us the right attitude for meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page xiii

page_content: May all living beings benefit from the dedication of those who have contributed to this process: Ma Thet (Moushumi Ghosh) for her tireless and dedicated retreat translations which are in this book, and for her careful telling of Sayadaw’s story in “A Note from the Teacher,” to all the folks who sent in transcriptions and those who did not shy away from our drafts in their roughest states.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 10, sentence number 4 on page xiii

page_content: A big thank you to Sheng Bin Chiu, Sajama Sajama, TJ, Jim Noyes, Tom Aust, Ashin Sunanda, Steve Armstrong, Douglas McGill, Martin Kaminer, Nancy Zan, Margaret Smith, Heidi Che, Chan Lai Fun, Tiffany Taalman, and Jie Zeng for joining us at different stages with your patient and close reading of the material.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page xiv

page_content: We have immense gratitude to folks globally who will be bringing this book to life with dana, layout, printing, and distribution including Chan Lai Fun and Hor Tuck Loon.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page xiv

page_content: May the reader benefit from this project as much as we have collaborating on it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page xiv

page_content: We assume responsibility for any errors in the transcription or editing and will do our best to rectify any issues for future editions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page xiv

page_content: Do email comments to info@wisdomstreams.org attention Laura.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page xiv

page_content: Finally, this book is by no means comprehensive, and not meant to replace Sayadaw’s personal guidance or your own further exploration of the Dhamma.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page xiv

page_content: Good luck in your endeavors!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page xiv

page_content: This book is dedicated to all the beings in the universe.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dear Reader, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page xiv

page_content:  With much mettā, Tony Reardon Laura Zan

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page xv

page_content: We work within the limits of language to describe the process of clear seeing that is at times difficult to pin down with words.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page xv

page_content: As such, some rules of grammar may be bent a teeny bit.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page xv

page_content: A construct like “the mind is knowing” instead of “the mind knows” is used in instances where it seems more appropriate to describe something in process.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page xv

page_content: The following words are used interchangeably: watching, being aware, observing, being mindful, recognizing, noticing, and paying attention.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page xv

page_content: You may also see the word “yogi” used often, which is Sayadaw’s reference to a meditator.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page xv

page_content: “Dhamma” with a big “D” refers to the teachings of the Buddha and the practice of meditation while “dhamma” with a little “d” refers to natural phenomena, natural law, or object.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page xv

page_content: We have tried to maintain Sayadaw’s voice as much as possible throughout.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page xv

page_content: The combination word “awareness + wisdom,” first coined by Sayadaw, but used freely before the book Dhamma Everywhere came out, underscores the need for more than mere awareness in vipassanā (insight) meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page xvi

page_content: Sayadaw always maintained that his teacher, the late Shwe Oo Min Sayadaw, referred to awareness with the assumption of inherent wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page xvi

page_content: We have also used the singular “they” as a gender-neutral pronoun where possible.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: On Language, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page xvi

page_content: Pāḷi and English words have been used throughout the book although we have limited Pāḷi usage to make this daily life book as accessible as possible.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 5

page_content: You may be finished with a formal retreat for now but remember that the dhamma is everything, so it is everywhere, all the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 5

page_content: Don’t lose track of that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 5

page_content: You can persevere and continue practicing at home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 5

page_content: Practicing the Dhamma includes all the practices of right thought (which is a fundamental part of right view), right speech, and right action.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 5

page_content: Practicing the Dhamma begins with a mindset change that, if we are able to take it on, may eventually lead to a lifestyle change.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 5

page_content: It is really worth the effort.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 5

page_content: The right efforts bear fruit when sufficient conditions are fulfilled.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 5

page_content: The Buddha said, “If you look after the Dhamma, the Dhamma will look after you.” Looking after the Dhamma simply means practicing it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 9 on page 5

page_content: After a certain point, the Dhamma will carry you along on its wave.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 10 on page 5

page_content: So keep going!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 5

page_content: What is dhamma?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 5

page_content: It is everything: all of nature, its causes and effects; the mind that is meditating (i.e.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 5

page_content: the qualities of awareness, perseverance, stability of mind, faith, and wisdom); it is everything that becomes observed in meditation (the sensations, the six sense objects which include the mind as a sense object, the experiences, the events, the thoughts, the pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral feelings) and also that which we may or may not consciously notice yet.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 6

page_content: These are all a part of our experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 6

page_content: By meditating, we are growing the qualities of awareness, perseverance, stability of mind, faith, and wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 6

page_content: These qualities when strong are called powers (bala) and they protect us, look after us, and bring us good results.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 6

page_content: So long as we are practicing, we are using the qualities of awareness (remembering what is wholesome), perseverance, wisdom, faith, and stability of mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 6

page_content: Over many years of retreats with my teacher, the late Shwe Oo Min Sayadaw (“Sayadawgyi”), I developed these powers, yet I did not use them in life and did not practice them except on retreat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 6

page_content: I had to truly dedicate myself to them before I understood what “Dhammaṃ saraṇaṃ gacchāmi: I go to the Dhamma for refuge” truly meant and what a true refuge the Dhamma truly is.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 6

page_content: I was about 13 years old when I started to meditate.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 6

page_content: I worked with a technique that required energetic, rhythmic breathing and got hooked on the pleasant physical sensation and calm that the experience brought.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 6

page_content: When I told Sayadawgyi about this and that I liked it, he was curious and asked me about my experiences.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 6

page_content: He would tell me stories of other people’s special meditation experiences and this would make me want such experiences and motivated me to meditate very diligently.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 7

page_content: I quickly caught on that when I maintained detailed mindfulness throughout the day, the feelings of calm and pleasantness could be sustained so I was zealous in maintaining mindfulness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 7

page_content: He also told me something that really stuck with me: be happy when I noticed anything about myself because it meant that I was aware of myself, that I was mindful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 7

page_content: I was so focused on the mindfulness that anything I noticed about myself became a source of joy: it meant I was mindful!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 7

page_content: It never mattered to me even if I discovered an undesirable quality because the point I saw was that I was mindful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 7

page_content: Sayadawgyi often asked me why certain things happened when I went to him to describe my experiences.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 7

page_content: One episode in particular was when he asked me to find out why the abdomen rises and falls (that was my main object at that time) and I said it was because of the breath.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 7

page_content: Then he asked why there was breathing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 7

page_content: I did not know this answer, which prompted a six-day search to find out.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 7

page_content: Every day, at every sit, the question dogged my observation of the breath and I remember on day six when the curious mind thought about why it was breathing and I saw the desire to breathe.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 6 on page 7

page_content: This episode and the success I had in finding the answer was so thrilling that I ran out of the dhamma hall immediately to tell my teacher the answer.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 9

page_content: It has fueled an eternal curiosity in me to observe and investigate, which of course has led to all sorts of discoveries.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 9

page_content: Many types of insights can be awakened.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 9

page_content: Insights into the nature of things include insights into the nature of our own practice, insights into the nature of awareness, insights into the nature of the things we observe and experience, insights into the nature of things as they are and more.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 9

page_content: Although we may not always realize it, just persevering and noticing is the main skill we need.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 9

page_content: Whenever awareness is established and strong, and we have an insight, we will begin to see nature in ways that we had never conceived of before and see things in ways we had never seen before.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 6, sentence number 5 on page 9

page_content: This is the growth of wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 6, sentence number 6 on page 9

page_content: Our work and our practice is always changing with this wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 9

page_content: Wisdom is one word but it encompasses so much and operates in many ways.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 9

page_content: Sometimes we miss the fact that it is there and doing its work because we are not familiar with the nature of wisdom and its various job descriptions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 9

page_content: We are more familiar with making an effort, like yogis making a physical effort not to be sleepy.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 9

page_content: Instead, when we are sleepy we could actually check out the sleepiness, if we are curious, and wisdom would at once be at work: Why is there sleepiness now?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 9

page_content: What has brought it on?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 7, sentence number 6 on page 9

page_content: What does it feel like?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 7, sentence number 7 on page 9

page_content: What we mostly do instead is resist this state because of our belief that sleepiness is not good when we are meditating.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 10

page_content: We react negatively as a result and try to do something immediately to fix the experience rather than seeing it as it is and checking it out.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 10

page_content: I recall my teacher’s first lesson to me: when I notice anything about myself, it means there is awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page 10

page_content: That is a good thing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 10

page_content: I also had a first taste of the importance of the mind after an incident with a bed bug.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 10

page_content: I loved the feeling of calm that came with continuous mindfulness and the attendant samādhi (stability of mind) so much that I would forgo sleep to enjoy this bliss.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 10

page_content: One night a bed bug bit me while I was meditating in my room in the middle of the night.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page 10

page_content: Even though this disrupted the blissful feelings, I was unperturbed.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 5 on page 10

page_content: I knew I could get it back so I continued meditating but it didn’t come back.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 6 on page 10

page_content: I tried harder, I walked, I sat, and I still did not sleep.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 7 on page 10

page_content: I became obsessed with regaining the blissful feeling, to no avail.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 8 on page 10

page_content: I continued to the morning, the whole day, the next night, the next day.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 9 on page 10

page_content: By day three I had a headache but I didn’t give up.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 10 on page 10

page_content: I put medicated plasters that relieve muscle soreness at my temples and continued my efforts, until finally it got too much and too painful, that I gave up.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 11 on page 10

page_content: I surrendered.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 12 on page 10

page_content: Never mind if I couldn’t have that bliss back, I thought.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 13 on page 10

page_content: I just dropped it and continued to meditate without any more desire to achieve that bliss.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 14 on page 10

page_content: It came back, just like that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 15 on page 10

page_content: As I simply paid attention to what I was doing, without a thought of having any more of the bliss, it was back.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 11

page_content: And I learned this huge lesson about meditation, which has stayed with me ever since, and I ran all the way to my teacher’s kuti (cottage) to tell him and he hammered home the bitter lesson, saying, “Well if you can get it by doing it, then do it.”

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: I didn’t know the mind directly then, but I just realized very deeply that greed was neither useful nor helpful when practicing meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 11

page_content: It had no place in getting me results.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 11

page_content: So I never had greed when I meditated anymore.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 9, sentence number 4 on page 11

page_content: Ironically that did not prevent me from having greed in many other aspects of my life, so much so that it led to depression.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: When I was around 18 to 20 years old on one of my retreats with my teacher, I remember Sayadawgyi mentioning that we can ask what the mind is aware of.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 11

page_content: Until then, in all my retreats over the years, although I was totally dedicated to being mindful on retreat, I simply watched my body movements and sensations.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 11

page_content: When thoughts were noticed, I acknowledged them then returned to the task of being aware of all my bodily movements and sensations, over and over again all day long.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 10, sentence number 4 on page 11

page_content: I recalled my teacher’s words the next morning as I was brushing my teeth and when I posed the question, it felt like a weight was suddenly taken off my mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 10, sentence number 5 on page 11

page_content: As it was, my practice had built up quite a bit of momentum on the retreat so when the attention turned to what the mind was aware of, and what the mind knew, there was suddenly so much to know!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 12

page_content: It took off!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 12

page_content: I saw the mind and whatever it knew.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 4 on page 12

page_content: From then on, my practice changed.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 5 on page 12

page_content: Whenever I went on retreat, I would, in sitting and in all activities, lead with the question: What is the mind knowing?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 6 on page 12

page_content: What is the mind aware of?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 7 on page 12

page_content: This opened up the practice immensely at the center.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 12

page_content: At home, the practice was forgotten and I got busy having fun with my friends.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 12

page_content: At University to study engineering, I became so involved in having so much fun with my friends that I flunked my second year of Regional College.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 12

page_content: At about this time, my oldest brother passed away from leukemia at the age of 43, and my father needed help running his menswear shop in a bustling wholesale center in downtown Yangon, a walking distance from our home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 4 on page 12

page_content: My father decided to take me out of University, no doubt a fact that I was a non-doctor in a family filled with brothers and a sister studying medicine.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 5 on page 12

page_content: The only other brother not studying medicine had already finished University and was helping my father with the shop.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 12

page_content: I wanted so much to continue to be with my friends that I actually, while helping out at the shop, still tried to repeat my second year at University on my own.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 13

page_content: It was not as much fun anymore by then because all my friends had already moved on to the main Rangoon University for their third year in Engineering studies while I was still at the Regional College.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 13, sentence number 3 on page 13

page_content: It was discouraging, so I stopped trying to repeat the year.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 13, sentence number 4 on page 13

page_content: I slowly descended into depression from resentment, fear, dissatisfaction, having to do what I did not want to, and not being able to do what I wanted to do.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 13, sentence number 5 on page 13

page_content: It was the love, or actually greed for fun that indirectly led to my years of depression.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 13

page_content: Initially I forgot about my unhappiness after a while, because it wasn’t very strong then and the depression seemed to go away.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 13

page_content: When it returned a second time, I got fed up of feeling mopey and angrily railed against the emotion and willed myself to overcome it that way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 13

page_content: While I was working with my father, I got more of an opportunity to go for longer retreats at my teacher’s monastery too, than when I had been in school.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 14, sentence number 2 on page 13

page_content: My father had no objections to my going and I mostly did the customary days off during the Burmese New Year.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 14, sentence number 3 on page 13

page_content: At one point I thought I wanted to be a monk for life and told my family and my teacher and they were all happy for me.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 14, sentence number 4 on page 13

page_content: Ten months in, my mind had changed again, and I just could not stay at the center any longer.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 14, sentence number 5 on page 13

page_content: My teacher gently tried to persuade me to stay but when my mind was made up there was no changing it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 2 on page 15

page_content: I went home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 3 on page 15

page_content: Ironically it was right on the way home in the taxi from that retreat that I actually descended into the deepest depression of my life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 4 on page 15

page_content: In hindsight, I think the feelings that came up had something to do with some insights into dukkha not being fully understood or realized.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 5 on page 15

page_content: Sitting in the taxi on the way home I began to feel irrational fears.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 6 on page 15

page_content: I was sure the policeman on the street knew about all my misbehaviors.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 7 on page 15

page_content: I went home fearful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 8 on page 15

page_content: This time I could not shake the feelings that overtook me and it was two long years of depression at home before I finally turned in desperation to meditation again, but this time I did not go to the monastery.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 9 on page 15

page_content: Circumstances did not permit.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 10 on page 15

page_content: My father was getting older and I could not take time off like I had done before.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 11 on page 15

page_content: I did try to take short holidays to places I had gone to with my friends before but nothing helped.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 12 on page 15

page_content: I could not be happy.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 13 on page 15

page_content: I only sought out meditation when it became my very last resort.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 15

page_content: When I finally, in my exhausted and suffering mind, decided maybe I should try meditating at home, something I’d never done before then, I went to speak to my teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 2 on page 15

page_content: Sayadawgyi just told me to try.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 3 on page 15

page_content: I accompanied my teacher for a four– to five–day retreat to a monastery about 10 miles out of Bago that he was visiting to give myself a boost.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 15, sentence number 4 on page 15

page_content: It was probably my time because I had three insights while meditating at that monastery that gave me some impetus to persevere when I went home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 2 on page 16

page_content: When I got to the monastery, I spent my time practicing as I already knew well how to.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 3 on page 16

page_content: The second day, I sat under a tree because there was not much to do out there, except to watch.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 4 on page 16

page_content: It was all trees, with just stillness and as I sat there I guess the silence turned the mind inwards.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 5 on page 16

page_content: Suddenly, I saw all the rage, resentment, fear, desires, and frustrations that were racing through my mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 6 on page 16

page_content: I was shocked.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 7 on page 16

page_content: I saw it was all negative thinking in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 8 on page 16

page_content: I realized if I died with the mind in this state, it was not going to a good state/place.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 9 on page 16

page_content: That was the first thing that made me afraid to die, not in general, but right then in that state.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 1 on page 16

page_content: The next day as I was taking a shower at a well, pulling up one pail of water after another and pouring it over myself in the heat, a man came running down a hillside.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 2 on page 16

page_content: He was large and very dark, his skin almost black, that he must have been in the heat a long time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 3 on page 16

page_content: He was sweating profusely and panting hard and he said to me as he ran towards me, “Water, water” and silently I handed him the pail full of water that I pulled up from the well.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 4 on page 16

page_content: He turned the pail up to drink directly from it and drank till all the water was gone!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 5 on page 16

page_content: Then he ran off.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 6 on page 16

page_content: I just stared at him.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 7 on page 16

page_content: Then it dawned on me after he left that I was all alone in that area.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 8 on page 16

page_content: The monastery had a lot of land.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 9 on page 16

page_content: My watch, which I had taken off, was on the edge of the well.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 16, sentence number 10 on page 16

page_content: I knew the area had prisons somewhere.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 2 on page 17

page_content: And the appearance of the man was not ordinary.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 3 on page 17

page_content: I got the chills.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 4 on page 17

page_content: I could have been robbed and killed if he had had any ill intentions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 5 on page 17

page_content: Again, the thought came to me that I was not ready to die in my current state of mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: Then the next night, in my last night with my teacher before I was going to battle it out on my own at home, he asked me to get him medicine from the dining cottage.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 2 on page 17

page_content: The monks’ kutis were far from each other and it was pitch dark but I knew the land well and there was moonlight so I walked without lighting the flashlight I had with me.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 3 on page 17

page_content: As I walked back to my teacher’s kuti at one point I just felt like using the flashlight so I flashed it and stopped dead.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 4 on page 17

page_content: Right in front of me was a viper.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 5 on page 17

page_content: One more step and my foot would have been struck.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 6 on page 17

page_content: My blood ran cold, the hair on my head (although shaved bald) stood and all I could do was stand.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 7 on page 17

page_content: I was so transfixed with fear that I could not move.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 8 on page 17

page_content: The snake moved away by itself but I remained there, standing still until my teacher sent his attendant monk to come find me and I followed him back to my teacher’s kuti.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 9 on page 17

page_content: I really felt the fear of death then.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 17, sentence number 10 on page 17

page_content: I was really afraid that I might die.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 18, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: Of course mindfulness was not continuous when I got home from that “kick-starter” retreat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 18, sentence number 2 on page 17

page_content: I knew how to watch the mind, I knew about the watching mind or awareness of awareness and I could use the six sense objects just fine, and although I often speak to yogis about using questions, I myself initially had none.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 2 on page 18

page_content: I was only meditating to calm the mind a little, as much as possible.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 3 on page 18

page_content: Fear and anxiety overwhelmed me, and when the feelings were really strong, I would sometimes use the feeling as the object and watch them continuously until the feeling subsided.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 4 on page 18

page_content: Other times, they wouldn’t hold my attention long enough and I used my breath to anchor my mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 5 on page 18

page_content: These objects of the feelings and the breath were familiar to me and I knew how to use these objects to anchor the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 6 on page 18

page_content: I did not begin using other objects until months afterwards.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 7 on page 18

page_content: Without attention on an object, the fear and anxiety could quickly become scary, and, if they became overwhelming, I used the inhaler because the sharp menthol smell and the sensation in my sinuses and forehead would bring my attention to these sensations and off the thoughts that fueled the emotions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 1 on page 18

page_content: The suffering feeling reminded me to be mindful and that mindfulness made me feel a bit better each time I was mindful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 2 on page 18

page_content: I visited my teacher every week or two and told him about my practice and complained about how hard it was to keep practicing at home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 3 on page 18

page_content: He would tell me it takes time to find our footing and encouraged me to keep trying.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 4 on page 18

page_content: My problem was not one of ability to practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 5 on page 18

page_content: It was more because the peace I got from meditation was not lasting.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 19, sentence number 6 on page 18

page_content: I already benefited from keeping up the mindfulness—it did give me relief from the suffering—but it did not give a lasting peace, which I craved.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 2 on page 19

page_content: I didn’t want to suffer and the temporary peace I got did not satisfy the mind’s craving.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 3 on page 19

page_content: It was demotivating yet I had to keep at it; otherwise, the mental suffering came back.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 4 on page 19

page_content: This in hindsight was a blessing in disguise!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 1 on page 19

page_content: I worked at my father’s shop, trying to cope with the demands of the business in my state of anxiety, uncertainty, and fear.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 2 on page 19

page_content: I often felt unable to make the decisions that had to be made.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 3 on page 19

page_content: Very often, the decisions just had to be made, without regard as to whether they were right or wrong, because my state of mind did not allow me this perspective.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 4 on page 19

page_content: Sometimes I would make the wrong decisions and had to deal with the fall out of that too.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 5 on page 19

page_content: Through it all, I would turn my attention to the suffering, or the breath as much as I could.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 6 on page 19

page_content: If the feelings were particularly harsh, I could neither face those feelings nor watch the breath successfully, then I turned to the mentholated inhaler.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 7 on page 19

page_content: The Vicks inhaler is a small tube with some mentholated ointment or oil inside that one can inhale through the tube.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 20, sentence number 8 on page 19

page_content: The sensation in the nose when I inhaled it would help to bring the mind’s attention back, and if I was really desperate I would inhale sharply to get a really strong sharp sensation that would get the mind’s attention, helping to gain control over getting mentally overwhelmed.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 21, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: During these initial two months I also began reading the books of Mogok Sayadaw.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 21, sentence number 2 on page 21

page_content: I happened to see my friend with one of a series of 36 books on Mogok Sayadaw’s teachings and eventually borrowed about six books over some months.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 21, sentence number 3 on page 21

page_content: Reading a few pages of these books every night, I eventually realized that Mogok Sayadaw was pointing to check to see how the mind is reacting to whatever it is knowing, over and over, constantly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: About two months after I began trying to practice at home and at work, the mind did begin to gain some balance.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 2 on page 21

page_content: Where I could not previously think without the cloud and background of anxious fears and doubts, I now began to be able to consider things more rationally and logically.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 3 on page 21

page_content: And one day I had a moment of clarity.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 4 on page 21

page_content: In a moment of balance in mindfulness I saw clearly that when there is mindfulness the mind is totally free of its suffering.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 5 on page 21

page_content: Then the suffering returned, but the insight had been strong.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 6 on page 21

page_content: Although it was just a glimpse, the mind saw this truth unquestionably.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 7 on page 21

page_content: There is a place of utter peace.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 8 on page 21

page_content: And the conviction that it gave the mind provided me with the motivation to continue practicing with increased vigor.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 9 on page 21

page_content: I knew now that this was the way out, for sure, so there was a real desire to practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 22, sentence number 10 on page 21

page_content: I began to think about practice daily, about how to practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 23, sentence number 1 on page 22

page_content: After about six months practicing at home, the depression had gradually disappeared and I began to notice that I would notice my reactions whenever I looked at people or when I heard something.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 23, sentence number 2 on page 22

page_content: The thoughts and feelings had calmed down enough and the practice had gained enough momentum that I finally had the mental space and interest to use objects outside of the breath or feelings.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 23, sentence number 3 on page 22
