page_content: The practice became interesting in and of itself, and I began initially just one at a time to explore the mind’s ability to stay anchored, for example on one pair of fingertips touching while sitting in the shop, on myself when drinking coffee or tea during a break, or being aware of seeing while waiting for customers, and so on.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 24, sentence number 1 on page 22

page_content: And these accumulated practices of collecting the mind enabled me to then be more aware of the interactions within my own mind when I was making decisions, counting money or attending to customers.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 24, sentence number 2 on page 22

page_content: I could notice when I lost my composure and devise a plan for maintaining it in my next interaction, always using the practice of collecting the mind as the basis for strengthening the mind’s composure.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 24, sentence number 3 on page 22

page_content: As these interactions happened daily and routinely, this is how I learned what helped me to maintain the composure of my mind, what helped me to remain aware and maintain continuity—when I needed to pick up attention on one object, mainly when I was no longer interacting with someone else or doing a task and when I needed to be aware in a more fluid way as in when customers approached and I had to attend to them and interact with them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 25, sentence number 2 on page 23

page_content: The awareness became closer to continuous and insights naturally arose more and more over time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 25, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: A year in, I read an article about the ājīvaṭṭhamaka sīla, eight precepts with right livelihood as the eighth precept.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 25, sentence number 2 on page 23

page_content: I was particularly interested in the expansion of the precept on not telling untruths, into four precepts for right speech: refraining from telling lies, slander, harsh speech, and idle chatter.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 25, sentence number 3 on page 23

page_content: I was inspired to try this.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 25, sentence number 4 on page 23

page_content: The depression had already made me quite subdued at the marketplace I worked at, compared to the joker I had been previously, and then practicing continuously made me quieter.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 26, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: However, taking on the four right speech precepts left me with hardly anything to say because I now avoided idle chatter.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 26, sentence number 2 on page 23

page_content: If someone came over to gossip about some other stallholder, they no longer found in me someone that sympathized.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 26, sentence number 3 on page 23

page_content: I merely listened and did not contribute as I saw neither wisdom nor meaning in adding fuel to their fire.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 26, sentence number 4 on page 23

page_content: The mindfulness became so much keener, aware of everything I said or planned to say and naturally the strength and composure of the mind also improved.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 27, sentence number 1 on page 24

page_content: After two years of practicing dedicatedly I began to notice that I had been avoiding unnecessary interactions and I began to challenge myself to go to social functions as a representative of my family and maintain mindfulness, or to go out for dinner with old friends and maintain my mindfulness, composure, or my stand without frustration or rudeness when they tried to get me to join them in drinking alcohol.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 27, sentence number 2 on page 24

page_content: I even went for a boxing match and learned a lasting lesson that this was not the place for me any longer.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 28, sentence number 1 on page 24

page_content: I tell you this story to say this is possible for you as well.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 28, sentence number 2 on page 24

page_content: You just have to persevere, experiment, and use your wisdom to figure out what works until this also becomes a natural part of your life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 28, sentence number 3 on page 24

page_content: I want to persuade you that this is possible.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 28, sentence number 4 on page 24

page_content: I really want you to know that you can do it at home when you have become skillful in this practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 28, sentence number 5 on page 24

page_content: This is my motivation to continue teaching.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 28, sentence number 6 on page 24

page_content: The only reason we aren’t able to bring it into our lives is because we don’t know enough yet or aren’t practicing enough yet.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 29, sentence number 1 on page 24

page_content: You may go on formal retreats to develop wholesome minds.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 29, sentence number 2 on page 24

page_content: But it is very important that you continue meditating outside of retreats because unwholesome minds arise in the absence of wholesome minds.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 29, sentence number 3 on page 24

page_content: Right now, as soon as yogis leave a retreat, they have no idea where all the wholesome minds have disappeared!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 29, sentence number 4 on page 24

page_content: It seems as if wholesome minds just arise on retreat, only to be replaced by unwholesome mind states as soon as a person returns home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 30, sentence number 2 on page 25

page_content: You have to cultivate your own energy and your own wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 30, sentence number 3 on page 25

page_content: If you are suffering, you will need to sit down and work out what to do about it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 30, sentence number 4 on page 25

page_content: If the parents always work things out for the child, the child will never learn for himself.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 30, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: Finally, we all have to live, but we must ask ourselves: Are we truly alive?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 30, sentence number 2 on page 25

page_content: If we don’t change and grow, it is akin to being dead.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 30, sentence number 3 on page 25

page_content: I encourage all of you to please continue practicing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 30, sentence number 4 on page 25

page_content: I make an aspiration for all of you to practice at home and to enjoy the fruits of your practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 31, sentence number 1 on page 26

page_content: When I know the mind is aware, there is a very strong feeling of goodness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 31, sentence number 2 on page 26

page_content: I greatly wish for yogis to understand and appreciate fully the value of awareness + wisdom and their role in helping develop all of the wholesome qualities of the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Note from the Teacher, paragraph number 31, sentence number 3 on page 26

page_content: This is the basic understanding that will carry the rest of the practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: When a retreat begins, defilements will negotiate with the yogi: “Okay, how long do you want to practice for?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 27

page_content: One month?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 27

page_content: Two months?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 27

page_content: Three months?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 27

page_content: All right.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 27

page_content: We’ll leave you alone for that long.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 27

page_content: But when the retreat is over, then it’s our turn again!” And it really is their turn again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 27

page_content: Delusion loves hearing the line, “The retreat is over!” It is very common to think that we’ve finished meditating.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 1, sentence number 9 on page 27

page_content: When this happens, it is now time for defilements to come back out to play.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: When I was younger, I went for long retreats of six to ten months at a time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 27

page_content: My mind cleared up while I was in retreat but I stopped practicing back home and my mind went back to square one.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 27

page_content: My life did not change.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 27

page_content: I never listened to my teacher’s advice to continue practicing at home and got into deep trouble!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 27

page_content: It was only when I was in the deepest trouble that I saw the need to work it out in daily life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 27

page_content: I began to practice continuously at home and realized the need to meditate all the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: When you return home, remember that you are there, at home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 27

page_content: You should remain in the present moment there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 27

page_content: The present moment is the only real thing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 29

page_content: Stay where you are because the now is always now, there is no “after now.” You’re trying to have mindfulness in this mind and the only effort is to remember to be mindful in the present moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 29

page_content: Through everything in your life you are remembering to be mindful, remembering how to be mindful, and building and maintaining samādhi, or stability of the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 29

page_content: You use the information that you have, combined with intelligence, logic, and reasoning to figure out how to practice correctly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 29

page_content: Next, you learn how to keep these already developed insights alive.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Mindfulness is a Lifestyle Change, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 29

page_content: Finally, you figure out how to develop even deeper levels of insight.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 29

page_content: We must be walking on the Noble Eightfold Path of sīla (moral conduct), samādhi (stability of mind), and paññā (wisdom).

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 29

page_content: This particular aspect of meditation, where you need to be practicing it all the time for it to be alive and beneficial becomes apparent to someone who practices continuously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 29

page_content: We really need to be a close friend of the Dhamma in order to grow in the Dhamma.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 29

page_content: It is when we are in such close communion with the Dhamma that we can grow in it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 29

page_content: Otherwise, there is not much growth.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 29

page_content: How can the Dhamma grow in us if we are not always living the Dhamma?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: The Buddha frequently reminded his followers to keep the Dhamma in mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: He didn’t say when to have the Dhamma in mind, but just said to have the Dhamma in mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 30

page_content: Unless we are always meditating, delusion will step in and hold the door open for all the other defilements to move in.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: People talk about how long they have been practicing meditation but they are only counting the length of time from their first retreat up to now.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: If they added up the hours that they were actually mindful, that would be a wholly different picture.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 30

page_content: The way we usually work is to put in a lot of effort over a small period of time in the hopes of getting some reward.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 30

page_content: That’s greed at work!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: Right effort is staying in it for the long haul.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: You need to become familiar with the work that you do, to always be in touch with it so that you become skillful at it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 30

page_content: Right effort is not letting it slack off, not giving up, and never stopping.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 30

page_content: Keep going.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 30

page_content: That’s real, right effort.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 30

page_content: Wisdom considers the long term.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 7 on page 30

page_content: There’s no hurry but we also do not rest.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 8 on page 30

page_content: The more we understand the forces of nature and how they work, the more we begin to rely on the laws of nature to let things unfold in a steady way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 9 on page 30

page_content: It is also not for nothing that I’m using this word nature over and over.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 10 on page 30

page_content: When something becomes your nature, really second nature, it means that the momentum of that nature is very strong in you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 4, sentence number 11 on page 30

page_content: It has become your nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 31

page_content: Momentum is important in every sphere of our lives and it is what makes things better and better.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 31

page_content: When there is momentum, it’s not only easier to do what we have to do but also easier to grow our expertise and improve.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 31

page_content: If you were a high jumper, you would run to gain momentum so you can jump over the hurdle.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 31

page_content: Right now, you may run towards the hurdle, look up, realize how high it is, stop, turn, and walk back!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 31

page_content: You didn’t have enough momentum to take the jump.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 31

page_content: I would ask you to look at your mind right now.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 31

page_content: How often do defilements come in unnoticed?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 31

page_content: You’ve got to turn that whole paradigm upside down so that what is natural and unnoticed and always going on in your mind is awareness, stability of mind, and wisdom, just as the defilements are present right now.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 31

page_content: If you ask me about setting aside time for meditation, yes, we should do that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 6, sentence number 5 on page 31

page_content: It is a time, whether we sit or use some other form, that we determine that we will have in mind to be mindful most continuously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 6, sentence number 6 on page 31

page_content: When I was at home, I sat before I went to work in the morning and before I went to sleep at night.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 6, sentence number 7 on page 31

page_content: I also sat from time to time throughout the day in my shop or at the coffee shop.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 6, sentence number 8 on page 31

page_content: When there was a lull in my work or I had 5 or 10 minutes, I would be still and do meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 6, sentence number 9 on page 31

page_content: All of these things gathered my mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 31

page_content: We haven’t reached the point where practicing continuously in daily life feels essential for us, so much so that we cannot live without it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 31

page_content: If we felt that way about practice in our daily lives, we would maintain mindfulness so much more continuously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 32

page_content: When we want to maintain the peace in our mind for longer and longer periods, we begin to realize that all these elements of knowing what we are aware of must become part of our practice and skill.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 32

page_content: Otherwise, we cannot maintain the peacefulness of the mind because we lose it when we lose awareness like a leaky faucet loses water.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page 32

page_content: We leak peacefulness when we lose mindfulness while talking, walking or going about our daily business.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 32

page_content: Let’s use a business analogy to think about meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 32

page_content: You secure some initial funding and you need to use your seed capital appropriately to start a business.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 32

page_content: So far, you have done retreats.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page 32

page_content: Once the cash starts coming in, you learn to maintain that cash flow.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 5 on page 32

page_content: In meditation, you now have to consider how to keep insights alive in your daily life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 6 on page 32

page_content: Do you know how to do that?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 7 on page 32

page_content: To keep insights from fading, you have to allow these understandings to arise over and over in your mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 8 on page 32

page_content: Continue practicing at home, as you would on a retreat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 9 on page 32

page_content: Once you can keep your insights alive and developing and keep your practice going, your business is now well established.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 10 on page 32

page_content: You can grow it even more.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 11 on page 32

page_content: You can dedicate yourself to expanding your business.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 8, sentence number 12 on page 32

page_content: If you do not, instead of being like entrepreneurs, you may be like day laborers who make just enough money to survive day-to-day.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: Does all this discourage you or propel you to press forward and go the distance?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: Sometimes it is hard to tell yogis the truth because I worry that I might make it all sound too difficult!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 33

page_content: (Laughs.)

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 4 on page 33

page_content: But it’s the truth.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 5 on page 33

page_content: Can you imagine how much practice that is going to take?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 6 on page 33

page_content: We want to turn the momentum of the current mind completely around so that it is going in the direction of wholesome momentum rather than unwholesome momentum.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 7 on page 33

page_content: Now, when we look, it is natural for liking and disliking to arise.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 8 on page 33

page_content: When we hear something, liking and disliking arises in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 9 on page 33

page_content: That is natural for us now.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 10 on page 33

page_content: Later, with more practice, it becomes natural for awareness + wisdom to be present when we look, hear, smell, touch, taste, or think.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEDITATING ALL THE TIME, paragraph number 9, sentence number 11 on page 33

page_content: Then we can begin to trust that awareness + wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: While I began practicing from a young age, I really started practicing at home in the midst of much suffering.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: I was in constant pain and sleep was my only rest.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 33

page_content: Every morning I woke up and the difficulty started again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 33

page_content: I did not communicate much at the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 33

page_content: The only question I asked myself at that time was, “Why is the mind suffering?” This question really motivated me to keep watching the mind all the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 33

page_content: At first it was just suffering and I didn’t know why.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 33

page_content: But slowly, from watching that continuously, awareness and samādhi grew and the mind became more peaceful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 35

page_content: There was less suffering.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 35

page_content: When the mind began to have more peace then I made peace the main object for my awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 35

page_content: Peacefulness became the anchor for my mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 35

page_content: Knowing the peaceful mind, I was also aware of everything else that was happening in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 35

page_content: I wasn’t interested in whatever level of wisdom I had achieved; I understood that if I were mindful, there would be some relief.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 35

page_content: I knew that whenever I meditated, the depression was reduced a little.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 35

page_content: I understood that much.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 35

page_content: I kept an eye on the peacefulness and on whether it was being maintained or disturbed with whatever contact there was with external or internal experiences.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 35

page_content: When you are aware of a peaceful mind, it continues to be peaceful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 35

page_content: It becomes more and more peaceful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 35

page_content: When you aren’t aware of the peaceful mind then the mind starts breaking down into chaos.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 35

page_content: How long can you maintain stability of mind in daily life?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 35

page_content: The Buddha said that when there are leaves covering the surface of a lake, you can’t see your reflection.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 35

page_content: Also when the water is turbulent, you can’t see your reflection.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 35

page_content: It is the same with the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 35

page_content: Stability and wisdom cannot arise when the mind is covered with defilements.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: We give wisdom more opportunities to arise when awareness and stability of mind are present for longer and longer periods.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 36

page_content: Once you recognize the agitated mind and continue to know that agitated mind, it starts to become peaceful and clear again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 36

page_content: Not being aware of the agitation or confusion only leads it to spiral into more agitation or confusion.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 36

page_content: When you know the peaceful mind continuously and you know everything that’s happening in the mind, you’ll also immediately notice when a speck of defilement comes to disturb that peace.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 6, sentence number 5 on page 36

page_content: At this point, you can immediately recognize the thought that preceded that shift from a peaceful mind to an agitated mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: Sometimes, even if you’re not watching a lot of objects, you can know the main object, which is the state of your mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 36

page_content: When you know the state of your mind, you also know the feeling, body, and mind simultaneously so there is no need to go after many objects.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 36

page_content: Instead, just know the state of your mind—whether it is peaceful or agitated.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 36

page_content: Keep that as your anchor as you know everything else and as you go about your business.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: Yogis want to improve the quality of the mind but if you haven’t seen the mind, the mind cannot change!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 36

page_content: In daily life, you may put in a lot of effort into your work with little energy left for awareness and awareness needs some energy as well.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 36

page_content: If you are not skillful at using just enough energy for awareness, you’ll use too much energy and won’t have enough to do normal work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 37

page_content: Learn to use just enough energy, and then practice continuously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MAINTAINING A STABLE MIND, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 37

page_content: You will become skillful if you do this every day.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BECOMING ENERGIZED, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 37

page_content: It’s very important to know the meditating mind that is at work because it allows us to see cause and effect and also helps us to continue doing the work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BECOMING ENERGIZED, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 37

page_content: It strengthens and energizes the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BECOMING ENERGIZED, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 37

page_content: Even when we observe that awareness is present, we must also be clear about the object it is knowing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BECOMING ENERGIZED, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 37

page_content: Both must be clear in order to have a complete picture of the workings of awareness in the present moment and thus for wisdom to arise.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 37

page_content: For most of us, awareness + wisdom are not ready and on standby so defilements are allowed to arise over and over.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 37

page_content: It’s a vicious cycle: we can’t get peace; then we can’t make inroads into the practice; so we can’t get peace.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 37

page_content: Even if we forget about wisdom for a while, we are not even able to really continuously maintain just awareness and stability of mind in our lives.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: Most of us become skillful at mindfulness of body and feelings but we don’t become skillful with mindfulness of the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: Particularly on shorter retreats, we do not become skillful in the third and fourth foundations of mindfulness—mindfulness of the mind and the dhammas.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 38

page_content: Short retreats give us time to calm our minds a bit and we go through the first and second foundations but we don’t really get established in the third or the fourth.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 38

page_content: Then when we go home and stop practicing, and we have to start all over again when we return for another retreat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: When we are learning to be skillful at something, like putting in golf for instance, we putt again and again and assess whether the energy we have used is sufficient.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: We then adjust our game and try again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 38

page_content: We need to bring that same approach to our meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 38

page_content: Instead of a forced focus, it is a light attention and full interest in the process.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 38

page_content: People who cook will know what I mean.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 38

page_content: How will an extra onion change the taste of this dish?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 38

page_content: How would it taste if we were to cook the onion a bit longer?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 8 on page 38

page_content: How would the taste change if we added soy sauce instead of salt?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 9 on page 38

page_content: When we do everything else in our lives, we know how to use our intelligence to figure out how to do something best.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 10 on page 38

page_content: Why don’t we do this with meditation?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 11 on page 38

page_content: Why do we just blindly follow instructions without getting personally invested and involved?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 12 on page 38

page_content: If we get an instruction to focus, we may just start doing it without considering whether it is helpful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 39

page_content: We need to assess for ourselves: Is this working?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 39

page_content: What is the effect?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 39

page_content: How is it?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 39

page_content: I want you to open the door to possibilities through your own understanding.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 39

page_content: When I first began practicing, my teacher would ask me questions and leave them unanswered.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 39

page_content: I wanted to know the answers so I would go and observe in my meditation with his questions in mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 39

page_content: As a result, I learned.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 39

page_content: At first, it was just greed in wanting to know, and that didn’t work!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 39

page_content: I then learned what did not work and what worked, and slowly found my way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 39

page_content: I was fortunate in starting young and having a very good teacher in Sayadawgyi.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 39

page_content: Skill and understanding of how to do right practice is very important.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 39

page_content: When we become skillful at observing all six of the sense doors and really have a handle on it, that’s when we find ourselves confident about practicing at home in our lives.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 39

page_content: We don’t know what circumstances are going to come up and we can’t predict what people or situations are going to hit us.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 39

page_content: I would liken mindfulness to a mother tending to a baby.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 39

page_content: Having children is a huge enterprise, as all mothers will know.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 39

page_content: I once observed a mother caring for her two-year-old and saw just how skillful she was with the toddler as he sat on her lap.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 39

page_content: She held a conversation with four other people while feeding the child.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 41

page_content: As the toddler moved in her lap, she adjusted along with him, and every now and then gave him some food, and if he didn’t like what she gave him, she would put the piece down and give him a different piece and later on pick up the original piece again to give the child.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 41

page_content: Sometimes she would eat it herself, always going with the flow, not making judgments of the child.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 41

page_content: Meditation is like this.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW DO WE MAKE INROADS?, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 41

page_content: We may find it difficult in the beginning to mind what we are doing and be mindful as well but it’s all practice and habits.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 41

page_content: If thoughts are too subtle, and you don’t catch the thoughts, then the causes will not show themselves.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 41

page_content: So there can be awareness and stability of mind, but wisdom is missing from the picture.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 41

page_content: There needs to be a little bit of inquiry.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 41

page_content: First, we are mindful of what is happening as we move around in daily life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 41

page_content: Then, later on, we’ll begin to notice why our limbs are moving like that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 41

page_content: There’s a reason behind it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 41

page_content: Whether it’s action, speech or mind, there’s some kind of idea behind these actions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 41

page_content: There is no speech or action without some idea fueling it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 41

page_content: Why are you wearing the shirt you are wearing right now?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 41

page_content: Why did you place your keys, for example, over here, instead of in the corner somewhere?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 42

page_content: There are ideas in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 42

page_content: Notice what is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 42

page_content: If you are at the point where you can be aware of the mind, begin to pay attention to how it is feeling or what it is thinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 42

page_content: You will then begin to see causes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 42

page_content: The mind is knowing, aware and feeling.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 42

page_content: Pay attention to the mind that wants to do things or that wants something to happen.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 42

page_content: These mental intentions are present everywhere in the body.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 42

page_content: What intentions are you aware of while you are walking?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 42

page_content: What are you aware of when you are sitting?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 42

page_content: What are you aware of when you are working?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 42

page_content: Do you notice the wanting to move?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 42

page_content: What do you want to move?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 42

page_content: Every movement involves mental intentions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 8 on page 42

page_content: If you’re moving both arms, there are intentions involved in both of them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 9 on page 42

page_content: You can see these things if you’re watching the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 10 on page 42

page_content: What are you aware of while you’re reading this?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 11 on page 42

page_content: If you only watch the body, you will not see a complete picture.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 12 on page 42

page_content: When you begin to see a more comprehensive picture of how the mind and body are operating, then you will also come to see causes and effects and it becomes very interesting.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 42

page_content: Defilements can still enter any time if there is awareness and stability of mind but no wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 42

page_content: So, even with the first two present, a little wrong turn in attitude can let in defilements!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 42

page_content: Fear can arise in just one thought.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 42

page_content: When you see one complete process, it will also be very clear that there’s no need to go around in circles.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 43

page_content: The mind understands that because of this thought, this effect of fear happens.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 43

page_content: A yogi who pays attention to the way these causes and effects work will come to own this knowledge of cause and effect.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: When I was trying to understand the emotion of anger, I asked myself whether I was happy or at peace when there was anger.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 43

page_content: Why was there anger?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 43

page_content: What was I angry with?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 43

page_content: When the mind is angry at something, there is some sort of underlying idea in there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 43

page_content: The mind is holding onto a certain belief and getting angry over it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 5, sentence number 6 on page 43

page_content: As soon as I saw this happening in the mind, the anger just slid away.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: Sometimes people don’t even have an idea of what they’re angry about.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 43

page_content: So many things have accumulated over time that it’s hard to pull apart the strands.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 43

page_content: It’s all jumbled up.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 43

page_content: I sometimes ask people why they’re unhappy and they don’t even know.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: You have to watch every time anger arises.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 43

page_content: This anger wasn’t here a moment ago.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 43

page_content: Look at this feeling very closely.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 43

page_content: It has something to do with the conditions surrounding your experience at this moment because the mind has certain presumptions about these conditions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 43

page_content: Is it a person?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 7, sentence number 6 on page 43

page_content: Is it something in the environment?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 7, sentence number 7 on page 43

page_content: Is it you?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: For example, people feel that they shouldn’t get angry when they’re on retreat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 43

page_content: Anger arises anyway.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 43

page_content: Now they’re angry that there’s anger!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page 43

page_content: This is possible!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: Yogis then get frustrated.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 44

page_content: Sometimes the mind may be in a habit of getting angry at something specific so when this object appears, anger will arise.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 4 on page 44

page_content: If you’re able to understand this as a natural phenomenon, then the anger will go down in intensity immediately.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 5 on page 44

page_content: How is that?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 6 on page 44

page_content: It’s because there is now Right Understanding.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: You want to watch and learn what and why something is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: You want to know why anger is present.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 44

page_content: Why does this feeling arise?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 4 on page 44

page_content: Maybe you are dissatisfied.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 5 on page 44

page_content: Why are you dissatisfied?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 6 on page 44

page_content: You want certain things to happen.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 7 on page 44

page_content: Let’s say you don’t get what you want or what you want to happen does not happen.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 8 on page 44

page_content: You want something.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 9 on page 44

page_content: Can you just watch this anger?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 10 on page 44

page_content: It’s impossible.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 11 on page 44

page_content: The anger is going to keep on going.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 12 on page 44

page_content: Why?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 13 on page 44

page_content: Because there’s some cause that you have not addressed yet.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 14 on page 44

page_content: If you want to know the intensity of the anger, you can look to the intensity of the wanting.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 9, sentence number 15 on page 44

page_content: They are related.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: When you have some preconceived notions about something and things don’t turn out the way you wanted them to, anger will arise.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: Or you may feel justified in being angry over something.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 44

page_content: You may be relating an event back to a friend and you might say, “You know I really ought to be angry about so and so…” What does this mean?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 10, sentence number 4 on page 44

page_content: It’s just the mind preparing itself to let this defilement loose.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: INQUIRY AS A PART OF PRACTICE, paragraph number 10, sentence number 5 on page 44

page_content: But it’s never good to let anger loose.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 45
