page_content: Now is the time for you to get into the boxing ring.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 45

page_content: I am the coach but all I can do is yell from the sidelines.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 45

page_content: For beginners, I’ll shout strategies when you look to me for support.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 45

page_content: Amateurs will often look to the coach for further instructions while the old hands don’t need to; they can think for themselves and deliver the punch.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 45

page_content: As a coach, I can yell what I want but you, as the yogi and boxer can only use the strategies you are familiar with.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 45

page_content: You are the boxer in the ring and you are facing the real deal.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 45

page_content: When I practiced at home, I would talk with my teacher once every week or so.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 45

page_content: If you are practicing at home and do not have anyone to ask, ask yourself the question then set it aside.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 45

page_content: Later, consider what I’ve said and what the Buddha taught.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 45

page_content: What have you tried before?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 45

page_content: What has worked?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 45

page_content: Should you try one way or another way?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 45

page_content: Even those who do not meditate will sit down and really think through a life problem if it matters enough to them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 8 on page 45

page_content: The thinking we are referring to with meditation is not just random, abstract thinking but right thinking and use of wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 45

page_content: My teacher never told me not to think.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 45

page_content: He didn’t exactly encourage me to think either, but he did ask me questions that required me to be aware and to reflect on how I was practicing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 45

page_content: I eventually discovered the answers and realized that one finds answers by being aware and by questioning.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 47

page_content: When you begin with the why, you are already bringing out your internal curiosity and intelligence.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 47

page_content: You’ll have many questions initially in the learning phase, but you won’t have that many in the wisdom phase.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 47

page_content: At that time, the mind will be clearer and less cluttered.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 47

page_content: The student is inside you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GETTING IN THE RING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 47

page_content: The teacher is also inside you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 47

page_content: This work is possible.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 47

page_content: You need to be patient and work through it for a few years continuously and patiently.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 47

page_content: You need to taste the full flavors of Dhamma: of knowing, of awareness, and of understanding.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 47

page_content: You all have to cultivate your own energy and your own wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 47

page_content: When you have tasted enough Dhamma, it will lead you along.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 47

page_content: When I practiced like that, I finally understood the meaning of if you look after the Dhamma, the Dhamma will look after you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 47

page_content: Or if you take care of the Dhamma, the Dhamma will take care of you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 47

page_content: When you really practice long term, the practice just becomes nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 1, sentence number 9 on page 47

page_content: When the practice becomes nature, it also becomes your nature to understand dhamma nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: Sometimes meditators are under the impression that they must practice in a specific way to get certain results.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 48

page_content: That’s not the case.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 48

page_content: The process itself is a learning process.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: “IF YOU LOOK AFTER THE DHAMMA, THE DHAMMA WILL LOOK AFTER YOU”, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: You are learning how meditation operates.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Take a Closer Look, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 49

page_content: My teacher constantly reminded me that all the problems in the world originate from the trio of lobha, dosa, and moha.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Take a Closer Look, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 49

page_content: Keep an eye on them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Take a Closer Look, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 49

page_content: Watching and keeping these unwholesome qualities at bay will allow the wholesome qualities to arise automatically.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 49

page_content: When there are lots of pleasant physical sensations, we may become greedy for more.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 49

page_content: Watch this greed while continuing to keep an eye on awareness as much as possible.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 49

page_content: When the wanting is very subtle and not detectable, you are at least still able to recognize the presence of awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 49

page_content: With an awareness of awareness, it is harder for the mind to get lost in the pleasant sensations.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 49

page_content: Additionally, having to work at being continuously aware makes it harder for greed to wallow in the feeling.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 49

page_content: Is a physical sensation of hunger in the stomach and wanting to eat the same thing?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 49

page_content: Without mindfulness, it becomes hard to disentangle these two and it seems like the same thing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 50

page_content: As you practice more, you will begin to recognize the differences between a bodily function like the stomach growling and the desire to eat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 50

page_content: When you are eating something, do you notice the difference between the taste of the hot-and-sour soup and the feeling that it is something pleasant?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 50

page_content: Hunger happens in the body or materiality whereas that desire to eat happens in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 50

page_content: That pleasant feeling is called vedanā.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 50

page_content: Lobha entwines itself around an object.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 50

page_content: Will it wrap itself around an object and then let it go or will it entwine itself around an object and think, “I can’t let it go, I need it near me, I want it all the time”?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 50

page_content: How strong is this lobha for this object?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 50

page_content: Let’s say we see a flower growing on a roadside and some people may note that it’s a lovely flower and keep walking while others may look at the flower and think that they want to keep it for themselves and pluck it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 50

page_content: Some attachments are like post-it notes while others are more like superglue!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 50

page_content: Let’s deconstruct attachment down to its nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 50

page_content: You may be attracted to a particular person and you think you like the person as a whole.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 50

page_content: What exactly is it that you like about this person?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 50

page_content: Do you like their eyes or hair?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 50

page_content: Do you like the way they behave or the way they smile?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 50

page_content: Are you attracted to a mental quality?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 7 on page 50

page_content: Do you have some idea about this person?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 8 on page 50

page_content: Are they kind?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 9 on page 50

page_content: Do they evoke a certain feeling that you do not get elsewhere?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 10 on page 50

page_content: What particular characteristic is the mind wrapping itself around?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 51

page_content: Delusion can be so strong that we are initially attracted to a particular characteristic but as we are trapped and move towards the object, we come to believe we like the whole thing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 51

page_content: Delusion spreads the view that this whole thing is fantastic!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 51

page_content: Only one or two characteristics reel us in and then delusion ensnares us whole.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE SPECTRUM OF WANTING, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 51

page_content: Check what catches your own mind and notice the experiences you are trying to recreate or re-experience through this particular product, person or experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 51

page_content: Everyone has a lot of craving, but there is one very strong, but elusive craving.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 51

page_content: It is the desire for everything in life to be okay.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 51

page_content: This desire for everything to be okay is very strong and powerful, but we are not aware of it because it hits us at the subconscious level.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 51

page_content: We grow up thinking that everything must work out the way we want it to and become upset when something tiny goes wrong.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 51

page_content: The mind immediately becomes frustrated at the slightest hiccup.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 51

page_content: For example, if we want 10 things and we get all of them, the mind calms down without a problem.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 51

page_content: If we can’t get one or two out of ten, the mind becomes agitated.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 51

page_content: If we can’t get half, the mind goes into depression.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 9 on page 51

page_content: If we can’t get any, the mind may go crazy.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 53

page_content: Don’t expect that everything should be okay.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 53

page_content: The future is open and there is a 50% chance that the things that we do not want can happen to us.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 53

page_content: We need to be prepared for this eventuality.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 53

page_content: What is obvious is that we will age, become sick, be separated from our loved ones and die.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE WISH FOR EVERYTHING TO BE OKAY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 53

page_content: Dukkha is certain.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 53

page_content: Let’s talk about aversion and the gamut of emotions ranging from sadness, sorrow, and fear, to hatred, anger, ill will, and other forms of aversion.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 53

page_content: Why is there aversion?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 53

page_content: Why is it happening?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 53

page_content: Whose aversion is it?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 53

page_content: Study this emotion as it arises and as it is happening in order to understand its nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 53

page_content: If there is some kind of understanding already about the nature of aversion, it is very hard for it to grow in intensity.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 53

page_content: When you are feeling angry about something, study all aspects of this anger each and every time it arises.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 53

page_content: Observe the feelings, thoughts, and anything else surrounding this anger.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 53

page_content: Observe the way the mind was thinking before this anger came up.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 53

page_content: If you become aware only when anger is already underway, or when it has passed, then you might not notice its causes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 53

page_content: You need to see the train of thought that came before this emotion.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 53

page_content: By this we are not talking about conceptual ideas (i.e.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 53

page_content: “so and so is making me mad”).

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 54

page_content: If you are aware of the mind directly while it is angry, you can see its causes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 54

page_content: Pay attention to the thinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 54

page_content: What is it thinking?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 54

page_content: How does it think when there is anger?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 54

page_content: How does the mind think in the absence of anger?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 54

page_content: With such awareness, you will begin to see causes and effects.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 54

page_content: So you see, it’s not enough to just to know whenever something arises.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 54

page_content: You have to reach the point where you recognize cause and effect at work as well.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 54

page_content: Without catching the causes, the effects will continue to grow unhindered and anger will grow and grow.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 54

page_content: Investigate these things and study the mind’s phenomena at work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 54

page_content: Recognize the internal chatter every time there is thinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 54

page_content: What kinds of thoughts are there when you are alone?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 54

page_content: What kinds of thoughts are there when you are with others?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 54

page_content: How does the mind think in these circumstances?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: WHY IS THERE AVERSION?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 54

page_content: You need to see all of these things.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 54

page_content: There was a time in my lay life when I had been practicing continuously for a year or so and I hadn’t had a major bout of anger for quite some time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 54

page_content: I woke up sick one day and I went to work anyway and managed the family business alone.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 54

page_content: Little dissatisfactions accumulated throughout the day but I didn’t take the time to work through them or clear them out.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 55

page_content: Later that night, I asked one of my younger brothers (a doctor) for some medicine and he responded dismissively.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 55

page_content: That was the final straw.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 55

page_content: I just exploded, thinking, “How dare he talk to me like this?” I kicked a chair across the room as I was used to doing in my younger days.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 55

page_content: My family was all sitting around the room at that time and everyone looked stunned.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 55

page_content: Awareness only kicked back in when my older sister asked, “What’s happening to you with so much meditation?” I then saw the whole process internally and externally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 55

page_content: I saw the frightened faces around me and realized how I had disrupted my surroundings.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 55

page_content: I also saw the whole mental process from a very tiny grain to this explosion.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 55

page_content: It became very obvious how negative this anger was.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 55

page_content: There was not one bit of wholesomeness anywhere.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 55

page_content: Seeing all of this in one big picture, I made a vow that I wouldn’t let something like this happen again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 55

page_content: I learned not to continue along a line of thinking when I saw dissatisfaction arising in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 55

page_content: Instead I would switch to watching the feeling associated with that thinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 55

page_content: From then on, I would clear the anger out even when I noticed a little bit of anger.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 55

page_content: Nothing else mattered except to pay attention to even the smallest hints of anger and I would work with samatha (tranquility meditation) and / or vipassanā depending on the situation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 55

page_content: Anger could go from a scale of zero to one and back to zero.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 56

page_content: I worked with zeros and ones, never letting it accumulate further.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 56

page_content: Ask yourself why when you find yourself getting angry at something.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 56

page_content: Question why you continue to be angry and why you are getting even angrier.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 56

page_content: There’s fuel somewhere.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 56

page_content: With many people, anger would go straight from zero to one to one million!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 56

page_content: Instead of cooling it with water, we’re all dousing this anger with gasoline!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: ZERO TO ONE MILLION, paragraph number 5, sentence number 6 on page 56

page_content: So whoosh, whoosh, whoosh, of course it would grow!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DELUSION, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 56

page_content: There are always subtle defilements underneath and delusion is always there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DELUSION, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 56

page_content: Delusion is there whenever wisdom is not there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DELUSION, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 56

page_content: It is only in those brief moments when wisdom is present that delusion is not present.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DELUSION, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 56

page_content: How many times does the idea of “I” not happen?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DELUSION, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 56

page_content: You may notice it for a brief moment then delusion comes in to cover the wisdom up immediately.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DELUSION, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 56

page_content: Moha is darkness and you have a thief’s tiny flashlight.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DELUSION, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 56

page_content: When you turn it on, it will light up a small area in front of you and you’re probably satisfied with that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DELUSION, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 56

page_content: You may think, “Oh, I know a lot now.” What about everything that you do not notice?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DELUSION, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 56

page_content: It’s vast and you only know what you know, but you do not know what you do not know.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: You don’t need to try to restrict or rein in a restless mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 57

page_content: Just recognize that if the mind is scattered, that it is scattered.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 57

page_content: The fact that you recognize it means that something is already at work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 57

page_content: Just know gently without getting lost in the thinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 57

page_content: The mind is like a monkey, going from tree to tree.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 57

page_content: How easy is it to control a monkey?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 57

page_content: Not so easy!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 57

page_content: Trying to control restlessness requires more energy and concentration, and will fuel even more restlessness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 9 on page 57

page_content: Remember that this thinking has nothing to do with you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 10 on page 57

page_content: You just want to know every time the mind thinks and be happy that there is awareness of this mental state.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 11 on page 57

page_content: When the right attitude comes in and you acknowledge that a scattered, restless mind is not a problem, then the mind will become more stable.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: If you know every time there is thinking, you (the meditating mind) don’t get drawn into the thinking, right?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 57

page_content: You will get caught up in the thinking when you don’t realize what is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 57

page_content: Instead of taking the storyline as your object, just know that the thinking mind has arisen.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 57

page_content: When one new thought has happened, you know.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 57

page_content: When another new thought has arisen, you know.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 57

page_content: Many, many new thoughts will arise.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 57

page_content: Then you begin to observe that the mind that you thought was yours is not yours.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RESTLESSNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 8 on page 57

page_content: The mind is mind, not “mine” or “me.”

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 59

page_content: It’s natural to have challenges arise in daily life and you may respond to these obstacles in different ways.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 59

page_content: You may solve the problem in the moment to make yourself feel better temporarily, but that brief relief won’t uncover the Second Noble Truth or the cause of suffering.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 59

page_content: Remember that there is no flyover or underpass on the Noble Path; you have to go through town with whatever experience that arises.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 59

page_content: The lessons are all laid out for you, and you have to recognize it as a lesson, a chance to work with the obstacle and come to a realization that can free you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 59

page_content: The real benefit of the Dhamma is in having looked at defilements and having understood them, to approach the same tasks with wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 59

page_content: We do not stop engaging with life issues.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 59

page_content: When the mind truly comes to understand something that is wrong as wrong, it will not repeat the same mistake again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 59

page_content: Understanding will then mature and you can handle life issues wisely.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 59

page_content: We want to realize that it is the mind and its machinations that make us unhappy.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 59

page_content: Some yogis tell me that they practice mettā to counteract judgments and comparisons but practicing this will not uproot the cause of suffering.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 59

page_content: Mettā is like a balm but does not take away the wound.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 59

page_content: It’s more useful when you recognize how painful it is to judge.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 59

page_content: The purpose of living with awareness is to really know how things really are and to understand.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 60

page_content: Hopefully that understanding will free us from those defilements.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 60

page_content: So next time, do not use mettā as an antidote for that because then you won’t see the truth of what is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 60

page_content: We should be interested in the relationships within us: the interplay of objects, the state of mind, wisdom, defilements, and awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 60

page_content: These are the relationships that we want to take care of.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 60

page_content: People out there and what they are doing are concepts.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 60

page_content: Moreover, objects out there are not doing anything but minding their own business.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 60

page_content: But this mind is not minding its own business and getting into all sorts of trouble.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 60

page_content: This is the relationship we want to take care of and this is what the practice is all about.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 60

page_content: Even if we were sitting somewhere and not speaking to or wanting to speak to anyone, we are still relating to our surroundings.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 60

page_content: Awareness should be observing this relationship.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 60

page_content: There is no need to fix the relationship that is already happening but we do want to be aware of what is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 60

page_content: There is a temporary end to suffering and there is an ultimate end to suffering and we have to pay the price accordingly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 60

page_content: Some things come easily and are cheaper.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 60

page_content: We will have to pay a higher price for something more valuable.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 62

page_content: Wisdom increases faith; anyone who understands the value of mindfulness and has benefitted from applying it in their lives sees their faith grow.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 62

page_content: The most important element of faith is faith in ourselves and in our ability to apply ourselves to the practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 62

page_content: Wholesome minds support each other just as unwholesome minds support each other.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 62

page_content: When there is more water, the fire

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GOING THROUGH ROADBLOCKS, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 62

page_content: goes out; when there is more fire, the burning continues.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 63

page_content: Daily life will be full of ups and downs when greed, anger, delusion, and all their relatives come on strong.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 63

page_content: The wisdom you’ve got may not be sufficient enough to stop them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 63

page_content: How do you approach such a situation?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 63

page_content: The first step is to accept whatever is happening in the mind as it is.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 63

page_content: Accept that this is just the nature of the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 63

page_content: Unless you accept, you will be fighting defilements with defilements.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 63

page_content: I draw the analogy of trying to accelerate a car with one foot on the brakes!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 63

page_content: The car won’t move.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 63

page_content: So, when you begin, the mind may not necessarily completely be open to this process.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 63

page_content: That’s fine.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 63

page_content: This is how you start when you’re developing your meditation skills.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 63

page_content: Learn from these different situations.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 63

page_content: Later on, you’ll begin to notice causes and effects.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 63

page_content: Understanding the causes that lead to greater momentum in the practice will allow your meditation to improve naturally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 63

page_content: While something is happening, awareness will continue to collect data little by little in the background and wisdom will step in when there is enough data.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 64

page_content: When wisdom steps in, you will no longer continue with the unskillful action.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 64

page_content: Acknowledge and work through these challenging situations instead of avoiding them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 64

page_content: When the mind comes understand that something that is wrong is wrong, it will not repeat an action again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 64

page_content: The lessons are all there for you to work through.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 64

page_content: What kind of understanding can you glean from what is happening?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 64

page_content: You can start by being interested.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 64

page_content: Then investigate.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 64

page_content: You will become aware of many different things but be mindful that these are just objects or experiences.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 64

page_content: Begin to ask more questions: What is a concept?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 64

page_content: What is reality?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 4, sentence number 7 on page 64

page_content: Continue to watch and learn.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 64

page_content: Cultivate curiosity and interest—they are important ingredients.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 64

page_content: Don’t be afraid of making mistakes, and never feel bad about having made a mistake.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 64

page_content: These false steps are the stepping-stones on our path and a part of our progress.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 64

page_content: We cannot avoid making them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 64

page_content: Becoming aware of, carefully looking at, and learning from mistakes is wisdom at work!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 5, sentence number 6 on page 64

page_content: As we learn from our mistakes, wisdom will start coming in more naturally, more automatically.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 5, sentence number 7 on page 64

page_content: Over the years, as our practice progresses, as we become more and more mindful, the knowledge and understanding we have accumulated will naturally come in more quickly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Reflect. Learn. Keep Going., paragraph number 5, sentence number 8 on page 64

page_content: Wisdom and mindfulness will start working as a team.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 65

page_content: The dhamma encompasses everything.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 65

page_content: All the good and all the bad are all nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 65

page_content: You may only want the good and not the bad but that’s impossible to have.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 65

page_content: You will have negative reactions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 65

page_content: When there is insufficient understanding into phenomena or the wrong attitude towards an experience, you will have a negative reaction and you can’t stop that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 65

page_content: Just know that a reaction is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 65

page_content: Know it and understand it, but don’t try and stop it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 65

page_content: Everything will fall into place once there is understanding.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 9 on page 65

page_content: Wisdom will know how to balance, do what needs to be done and drop the unnecessary bits.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 10 on page 65

page_content: Don’t force something to happen.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 11 on page 65

page_content: This goes against nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 12 on page 65

page_content: While you can’t get something just because you want it or you work for it, there are two things that will move us forward: how much you do and how skillful you are.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 13 on page 65

page_content: Pay attention to applying skill and right effort— these pieces deserve your attention.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 14 on page 65

page_content: Meditation is not like the lottery; you don’t pay a little and win a huge windfall.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 65

page_content: Reducing defilements is a learning process, and you will find that your understanding slowly emerges and moves toward the middle path as you recognize the extremes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 65

page_content: Suppressing and expressing are the two different extremes, but you will experience them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 65

page_content: Watch what the mind does in those moments, learn what is beneficial and adjust accordingly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 65

page_content: If you think something is good, you’re at one extreme, if you think something is bad you’re at the other extreme.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 67

page_content: You have to see things as they are and where they lie.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 67

page_content: Therein lies the Middle Way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 67

page_content: For example, when a defilement arises in the mind, you neither try to stop nor encourage the defilement.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 67

page_content: Exercise right view in remembering that this defilement is not you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 67

page_content: The defilement will express the amount of power it has and you can’t prevent that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 67

page_content: Just step back and keep an eye on the defilement, consistently and patiently, learning and figuring out how it functions and its job description.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PAY ATTENTION TO SKILL AND RIGHT EFFORT, paragraph number 3, sentence number 8 on page 67

page_content: This is a learning process.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 67

page_content: How can we view the world through the lens of Dhamma?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 67

page_content: When we are thinking about the world, we are already thinking about concepts; we are thinking about people, places, and situations.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 67

page_content: When the mind pays attention to concepts, either wholesome or unwholesome states of minds will come up depending on how much wisdom we have.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 67

page_content: I’ve noticed that when yogis observe defilements, it is often from a point of view that they are enemies to be fought.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 67

page_content: That is already a battle lost.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 67

page_content: Defilements like it when you fight because fighting in itself is a defilement and they like that they have a spy in your camp.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 67

page_content: Fighting anger with anger gets you more anger, or “anger-squared.” Defilements cannot stand it when you watch them calmly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 68

page_content: When you accept a defilement’s presence, take interest in it, and try to understand it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 68

page_content: That’s when the defilement becomes really uncomfortable.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 68

page_content: For me, I understood that difficulties would decrease if I practiced mindfulness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 68

page_content: I noticed that the intensity of suffering and problems would decrease when I practiced.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 68

page_content: Truly understanding this motivated me to continue.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 68

page_content: When real understanding is present and strong, the mind changes for the better.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 68

page_content: If wisdom isn’t strong enough, the mind can’t change because defilements still arise.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 68

page_content: There are people who know about meditation but they don’t practice; for these people, their minds do not really change.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 68

page_content: Depression drove me to practice wholeheartedly at home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 68

page_content: I remember first trying many different ways to alleviate these feelings.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 68

page_content: I went everywhere that I had ever been and explored new places seeking happiness but the depression followed me everywhere.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 68

page_content: I couldn’t find happiness when I went out with my friends, whether I went to the beach or to the mountains or when I went thrill-seeking somewhere.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 68

page_content: Nothing made me happy.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 68

page_content: Even drugs couldn’t do the job.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 7 on page 68

page_content: It finally occurred to me one day that even if I were to go to the moon, the depression would also follow me there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 69

page_content: During this period in my life, my suffering became a very obvious object of meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 69

page_content: I watched until it became unbearable and then I changed the object to something neutral to continue being aware.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 69

page_content: In Myanmar, ānāpānasati (awareness of the breath) was used as a common, neutral object but it was too subtle for me at the time because the suffering was so strong.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 69

page_content: I needed something stronger to make my breath obvious.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 69

page_content: I found the answer in a Vicks nasal inhaler because I found that when I used the inhaler my attention would be at my nostrils.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 69

page_content: When it comes to using wisdom, I’m not asking you to think of a solution or to resolve the situation by thinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 69

page_content: That’s not what I’m saying.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 69

page_content: Thinking a little allows us to practice effectively.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 69

page_content: I see it as a considered practice where you are aware and you reflect a little bit on what you’re doing, how you’re doing it, and what you’re discovering: a little bit, not a lot.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 5 on page 69

page_content: This is what we do when we are at work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 6 on page 69

page_content: When we do our work, we consider whatever work we have done before and how we accomplished this kind of work so we can figure out the best way to move forward.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 7 on page 69

page_content: None of us do work without considering how to do it the best way possible.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 8 on page 69

page_content: Even when we’re trying to fix something we are not yet familiar with, we may tinker with it, take time to reflect, check what is happening, see whether it is working and then tinker a bit more.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 9 on page 69

page_content: That is what I mean by using wisdom in using our own intelligence to find our way in our own practice.
