metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 70

page_content: If any of the Brahma Vihāras, or the “sublime states” of loving kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity arise, the mind will feel good and we will feel at peace and ease in understanding of the way things are.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 70

page_content: If there is compassion without wisdom, that compassion might actually be contaminated because it is mixed up with a non- acceptance of what actually is; a feeling of helplessness will be in there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 70

page_content: If that happens, you will need to clear the mind first.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 70

page_content: Come back to observing the unwholesome mind state with right view.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 70

page_content: Do not try to force yourself to have equanimity.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 6 on page 70

page_content: Work with the unwholesome states that arise until the mind comes to some balance.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: HOW TO WORK WITH DIFFICULTIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 7 on page 70

page_content: Then you will recognize whether it is any of the Brahma Vihāras arising in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 70

page_content: When I was deep in depression and I began to try practicing at home initially, I watched all my difficult emotions and it became a very strong motivation to keep awareness in order to get better.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 70

page_content: As soon as it got better I would lose the motivation to be as mindful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 70

page_content: I would revert back to my old ways, talking and joking with neighbors and neighboring shops or customers.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 71

page_content: Then some trigger would hit me really hard, or hit a sore point that brought up all the old anxieties, fears, guilt or shame and I became overwhelmed again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 71

page_content: Watching all the old fears and feelings would alleviate it a bit.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 71

page_content: But when it got better, I forgot again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 71

page_content: It felt like people were coming by and slapping or hitting or punching me mentally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 71

page_content: It was not intentional on their part; it was just that my mind was not strong enough to be resilient.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 71

page_content: Over many months, seeing the mind triggered daily and easily, I began to think about why I kept going back into this cycle.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 8 on page 71

page_content: I suffered through this many, many times.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 9 on page 71

page_content: I then realized that I was becoming too relaxed and not being mindful enough.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 10 on page 71

page_content: When I began to feel better, I thought, “I can’t stop being mindful.” Even when I was feeling better and there was nothing to do, I would not waste my time talking needlessly, and I would always be mindful of something or another.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 71

page_content: I became prepared when people came to talk to me.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 71

page_content: Other people can be full of defilements too and they come and unload their defilements on us.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 71

page_content: If we are not ready, we get affected and infected.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 71

page_content: Even with family members, I began to realize that I could not just relax.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 71

page_content: I could not drop mindfulness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 71

page_content: My mindfulness got better and more continuous.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DON’T LET GO OF MINDFULNESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 71

page_content: This is how we learn from life—our failures and difficulties teach us.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 73

page_content: My teacher would always ask how I was practicing and that is what I’m asking you now.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 73

page_content: “What are you doing?” or “What is special or different today?” were questions my teacher would ask me.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 73

page_content: Yogis who are practicing will know the answer while those who are not practicing will not know.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 73

page_content: Reviewing what goes on in the mind is the work of wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 73

page_content: At first, when we watch anger, we are just watching.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 73

page_content: Reviewing what has happened sets a direction for the mind and creates a map of sorts for future awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 73

page_content: You have a situation that you consider from different angles and decide to try meditating a certain way the next time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 73

page_content: When the next time comes, you are more likely to remember to try it that different way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 73

page_content: If you forget, set an intention to try again the next time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 73

page_content: When you play a game with the same scenarios occurring again and again, you become an expert at knowing ahead of time what moves are beneficial and what are not at certain junctions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 74

page_content: If you want to improve, you first work continuously without a break.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 74

page_content: You also use the wisdom you’ve got, determining what is skillful and what is not skillful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 74

page_content: You need to assess your practice for yourself.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 74

page_content: Like running a business, you’ve

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: REVIEWING WHAT HAS HAPPENED, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 74

page_content: got to be savvy and learn from your practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 75

page_content: When you are aware of what’s happening in the mind, you begin to notice the undesirable parts and will want to change.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 75

page_content: This is a beginning.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 75

page_content: The lessons are all there for you to go through and learn.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 75

page_content: Later on, if you really come to understand that something that is wrong is wrong, you will not repeat it again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 75

page_content: Don’t try to do anything,

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 75

page_content: don’t try to prevent anything,

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 75

page_content: but don’t forget what is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 75

page_content: This is what my teacher, Shwe Oo Min Sayadaw would say as his instructions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 75

page_content: In the first two lines, he is speaking to the experience of what is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 75

page_content: Awareness can be at work, but you do not try to alter the experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 75

page_content: You let the process continue as it is without interfering.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 75

page_content: Let things unfold naturally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 5, sentence number 6 on page 75

page_content: Your job is to watch, know, learn, and gain experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 5, sentence number 7 on page 75

page_content: As you watch an experience continuously, you’ll begin to recognize patterns within it and later see the whole picture.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 76

page_content: The value of this meditation becomes more apparent with dedicated practice over long stretches of time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 76

page_content: The more experienced you become, the deeper you will see.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 76

page_content: The dhamma is very subtle and you’ll see this when you practice long term.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 76

page_content: What is happening in the mind and what is happening in the body right now?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 76

page_content: How is one mind or mental state related to another mental state?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 76

page_content: You want to be interested in the nature of this mind and body.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 76

page_content: Hold concepts or story lines on the side for now.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 5 on page 76

page_content: As wisdom gains traction and defilements eventually thin out, you’ll see that there aren’t that many problems per se.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 6 on page 76

page_content: “Problems” arise when there is a lot of greed, aversion or delusion present.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 6, sentence number 7 on page 76

page_content: When we can’t accept things as they are, we exhaust ourselves with desire for things that are not present or with desire to push away things that are already there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 76

page_content: The mind seeks variety, novelty and freshness and becomes lazy or bored when it’s not satiated.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 76

page_content: If we think that a certain experience is lingering longer than we’d like, we’re not observing properly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 76

page_content: No two moments are the same.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 76

page_content: Every moment is fresh and every moment is changing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Day-to-Day, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 76

page_content: Even if an experience may seem neutral, we can still see subtle changes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 77

page_content: Yogis usually know about the Five Precepts and Eight Precepts so I do not need to tell them to you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 77

page_content: If there is always mindfulness, you will automatically follow the Five Precepts.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 77

page_content: We must have some idea about what the Five Precepts entail in order to maintain them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 77

page_content: It’s important to have that kind of knowledge already, but I’m not the sort of person who wants to impose requirements on people.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 77

page_content: If you merely follow my instructions without conducting your own investigation, wisdom won’t arise in you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 77

page_content: You need to learn to the point where you personally understand why you need to act in certain ways.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 77

page_content: There are two aspects of sīla or moral conduct: the doing of what should be done, and the not doing of what should not be done.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 77

page_content: If you know the Pātimokkha (the Buddhist monastic code), the Buddha says to do good, avoid evil, and purify the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 77

page_content: The Buddha says to do what should be done.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 77

page_content: The Five and Eight Precepts that we always talk about is abstaining from that which should not be done.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 77

page_content: Remember that there is also the other side: do as much as possible of what should be done.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 77

page_content: If we are being mindful all the time and wisdom is growing, we begin to understand what should and shouldn’t be done, because we will observe the effects of our actions and learn from that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 77

page_content: In my experience, my behavior changed through continuous practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 79

page_content: I became very calm and spoke very little or not at all if it was not important.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 79

page_content: I was serious when I did talk.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 79

page_content: Being very careful in speech became important in my practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 79

page_content: I practiced refraining from lying, slander, gossip, talking nonsense, speaking harshly, and idle chatter.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DOING WHAT SHOULD BE DONE, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 79

page_content: And as a result, my meditation deepened.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 79

page_content: Every person acts largely out of their own past causes and conditions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 79

page_content: People brought up in Asia have a certain cultural conditioning and people brought up in the Americas or Europe have their own kind of conditioning.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 79

page_content: Then there’s individual conditioning, parental influences, and each person’s experiences throughout life; there are so many combinations and permutations that everybody has a unique footprint.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 79

page_content: ‘Non-self’ is just that principle of cause and effect.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 79

page_content: For example, somebody may have a very hot temper, and that person’s conditioning includes certain triggers that sets off their temper.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 79

page_content: If you understand that a person’s past conditioning is what is causing them to lose their temper as opposed to believing that they personally have negative intentions towards you, then it becomes easier to see the process and forgive that person.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 80

page_content: You recognize that because of conditions, the results arise, and it’s not the person who’s angry, but a set of conditions that has brought about this effect.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 80

page_content: If you stay in the present moment, moment to moment to moment, you see the principles unfold.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 80

page_content: If you are angry and you watch that anger many times, you will begin to notice how the anger cannot run away with you as long as you are aware.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 80

page_content: You begin to see how, with awareness + wisdom, the unwholesome mental states cannot take over the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 80

page_content: Without awareness + wisdom, the unwholesome mental states can gain momentum unbeknownst to you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 80

page_content: When there is knowing, you begin to notice how the habit of anger could arise in the absence of awareness or wisdom and how the process unfolds naturally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 80

page_content: It’s all about mental processes; it’s never about the story or external things.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 80

page_content: When you understand the benefit of something, the harm of the opposite becomes obvious.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 80

page_content: I say this a lot: when yogis successfully observe something unwholesome and it passes, and they are happy.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 80

page_content: But that’s not the end.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 80

page_content: It is important to reflect at that point what would have happened if we had not been mindful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 80

page_content: If we are just happy in la la land we don’t get to learn what it is like to be unmindful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 81

page_content: I usually tell yogis to stay away from the storyline, but it can help to see the idea that the story carries.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 81

page_content: The story might be, “Anne was making a noise and I felt really angry with her” while the idea behind that might be “people shouldn’t make noise.” This idea influences the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CAUSES AND CONDITIONS, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 81

page_content: But when it is seen clearly for what it is, that this idea is not helpful, then it can be let go.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 81

page_content: We get into a lot of trouble when there is attachment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 81

page_content: When you or the other person is clinging to each other or to the relationship, double trouble!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 81

page_content: For example, thinking that you will be okay only if your children are okay implies that the mind is reliant on somebody else for its mood and that takes away the mind’s freedom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 81

page_content: This is a strong delusion.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 81

page_content: With attachment come anger, anxiety, fear, and doubt but if there’s wisdom there can be loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 81

page_content: When the mind doesn’t believe that its suffering comes from other people, situation or places, then it is free.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 81

page_content: An old couple was once asked how their relationship had lasted 50 years.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 81

page_content: One of them replied that they made adjustments every time their communication broke down.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 81

page_content: It worked because that adjustment was not made with passion or defilement but with wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 82

page_content: We become strangers to each other in a family if we don’t talk to each other for long periods of time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 82

page_content: It takes a lot of wisdom to know whether and when to speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 82

page_content: Consideration of time, place, and other factors are all a part of that moment and situation before we can decide whether it is appropriate to say something.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 82

page_content: Sometimes it may be necessary to use humor to ease a situation; jokes and humor are very attractive to people.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 82

page_content: So, love as much as you can but do not get attached.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 82

page_content: What emotions come up when you think about someone you love?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 82

page_content: If there is only love, there is only happiness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 82

page_content: But love can get mixed up with a bit of attachment and that’s followed by fear.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 82

page_content: Loss is a natural part of life so there will be loss eventually.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 82

page_content: We need to understand it instead of fearing it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: RELATING TO OTHERS WISELY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 82

page_content: Every day we are losing time, losing the object, losing awareness, in every moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 82

page_content: We must be aware of ourselves every time we speak; come to know the many steps the mind goes through to speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 82

page_content: If yogis make a habit of knowing themselves, then awareness when speaking will naturally become part of that habit.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 83

page_content: For some time I have had yogis not speak for two-thirds of a retreat and then, in the remaining one-third I explain how to be mindful while speaking and get the yogis to try mindful speech for a few days.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 83

page_content: Ideally yogis will have built up enough momentum and continue being mindful while speaking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 83

page_content: But there is generally some inability or misunderstanding of how to remain aware when talking, and after a while I notice yogis speaking without being mindful and their momentum is gone.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 83

page_content: I now limit the practice to just one session to give just a taste of what it might be like to speak mindfully.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 83

page_content: I did not invent this idea of cultivating awareness while talking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 83

page_content: It is nothing extraordinary.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 83

page_content: The Satipaṭṭhāna Sutta prescribes, “When silent, be silent with awareness and wisdom, when speaking, speak with awareness and wisdom,” but few people practice that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 83

page_content: It’s difficult to practice being mindful when speaking and seeing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 83

page_content: The reasons are that we don’t practice being mindful when speaking, seeing, and looking and we also don’t have enough momentum in our awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 83

page_content: The whole point of my retreats is that I’m teaching you a skill that you’re supposed to bring into your life, and use in your life, so that mindfulness becomes a way of life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 83

page_content: That’s why I think it is useful to know and practice being mindful when you speak, especially when you are back home and at work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 85

page_content: Awareness of speech is not a strange or wonderful thing but something very simple: be mindful when you speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 85

page_content: Some people ask, “What should I be aware of?” and I say, “Know that you are speaking, notice whatever part of the speaking experience is most obvious to you.” On retreat we practice observing body sensations and feelings, and observing the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 85

page_content: All these things are still present when we speak and we want to know which of these catches our attention as we speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 85

page_content: The biggest trap when speaking is the strong desire to speak, wanting a lot to speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 85

page_content: A strong desire to speak is greed and mindfulness then goes out the window.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 85

page_content: We can’t practice awareness of speech alone; we have to speak to somebody.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 85

page_content: We have to pay attention to an outside object, not just our own mind and body, so we lose mindfulness because our attention goes fully outside and then we are no longer attentive internally or to our speech.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 85

page_content: We start to think about the other person and look at the other person and then all our attention and our awareness goes outwards and our attention is no longer also directed toward ourselves.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 85

page_content: On the other hand, practicing talking meditation too formally and only in paying attention to one self will make the conversation stiff and unnatural.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 85

page_content: A yogi said in a Finnish retreat that while the retreat is quite good for mindfulness, there’s a lot of stuff happening and especially in speaking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 85

page_content: When speaking with others, a yogi has to be responsive and quick and it feels like they’re doing two jobs at once.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 86

page_content: Yes—it will be this way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 86

page_content: It’s all about skill, and skill is gained from continued practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 86

page_content: We will be slow in any subject when we are not skillful as yet.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 86

page_content: As we do it over and over again, we gain momentum and the process becomes easier.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 6 on page 86

page_content: While there may be a lot of personal effort in the beginning, it eventually begins to roll on its own momentum, like riding a bike.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 7 on page 86

page_content: I liken it to martial arts, where at first you are practicing some simple moves and after a while (you don’t know when), you can just use them automatically.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 8 on page 86

page_content: In this same way, the mind may now be a little slow and deliberate when it begins this practice, but as you keep doing it, you will find it slowly becoming automatic.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 86

page_content: You could try practicing in this way: Know what you want to say.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 86

page_content: Think about what you want to say before you say it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 86

page_content: When you acknowledge to yourself first, to what you feel and think before you speak, you are giving yourself time to know yourself before you speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 86

page_content: Try this over and over again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 86

page_content: You can be aware of your experience as you are listening to the other person and recognize that you are listening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 7, sentence number 6 on page 86

page_content: You are listening and knowing and are aware that you are listening and they are listening and then you are aware that the act of listening is happening, that speech is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 87

page_content: You might have to practice this quite actively; it will be difficult at first but only because you haven’t had enough practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 87

page_content: Every new thing you learn is difficult when it is new because you don’t have enough practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 87

page_content: When your overall mindfulness becomes more natural and continuous and has some momentum through practice and you know the mind and recognize it at work, it becomes easier to notice when you are talking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page 87

page_content: You will notice when the mind is thinking, when it is listening, when it is hearing, looking, and so on.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 8, sentence number 5 on page 87

page_content: You will need to accept that at first you will lose mindfulness quite a lot.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 8, sentence number 6 on page 87

page_content: Just don’t give up.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 87

page_content: Before I learned how to practice in social situations, I would spend as much time as I could by myself and did concentration practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 87

page_content: The mental stability was there but I couldn’t find peace because I was resistant to social situations.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 87

page_content: I wasn’t skillful but tried over and over again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 9, sentence number 4 on page 87

page_content: As I learned to practice with people around, slowly over a long time, the mind’s habit changed; my awareness increased and slowly became automatic.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 9, sentence number 5 on page 87

page_content: When I saw that my resistance was because of conditioning, I actively set out to practice in social situations to overcome the resistance.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 87

page_content: At one point, I decided to practice right speech, meaning no lying, no harsh speech, no slander, and no idle talk.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 87

page_content: Taking on this task really helped me in talking meditation because I had to check if I was doing any of the four wrong speeches.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 88

page_content: It reduced the amount of talking tremendously and my practice just shot up because we speak so often and I had to be so mindful all the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 88

page_content: I don’t remember how long I practiced actively like this but after some time of practicing I could maintain my peacefulness while I was with others.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 88

page_content: After I was more settled, I actively increased the practice and sought out places that disturbed me because I knew that’s where my weakness was.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 88

page_content: I would go to those places and keep trying there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 11, sentence number 4 on page 88

page_content: Sometimes I would walk into a bar and meditate.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 11, sentence number 5 on page 88

page_content: I wanted to know how much this affected my mind and I became really interested.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 11, sentence number 6 on page 88

page_content: Everyone around me was drunk and talking loudly but I was very peaceful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 11, sentence number 7 on page 88

page_content: This is how I developed confidence in my practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 88

page_content: It’s good to know, as information, that, it is possible to be mindful when you speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 88

page_content: Then you are willing to try and put this aspect of mindfulness practice into your daily life, to challenge yourself to use this as part of your practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 88

page_content: It is very easy for defilements to come into our speech when we speak about ourselves, when the “I” becomes involved.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 88

page_content: At home we usually speak without mindfulness and it is this habit, rather than mindfulness, that tends to come in when we speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 14, sentence number 2 on page 89

page_content: When we are speaking, there are three things we can be mindful of: our mind, our speech and our body.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SPEAKING MINDFULLY, paragraph number 14, sentence number 3 on page 89

page_content: If we know our mind then we should definitely be able to filter that into knowing our speech and our body as well.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 89

page_content: You need to have your eyes open while meditating in daily life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 89

page_content: I learned how to be aware with my eyes open because fellow shopkeepers used to tease me when I meditated at work with my eyes closed.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 89

page_content: They thought I was sleeping on the job!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 89

page_content: So I learned to pay attention to the internal landscape while keeping my eyes open.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 89

page_content: I eventually became more skillful at knowing the difference between seeing and looking, and the people I worked with didn’t even know that I was meditating.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 89

page_content: If you actively know that you are aware and you are tuned into this awareness, then it’s hard to miss the seeing that’s happening in this moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 89

page_content: One exercise you can do to help recognize this is to sit with your eyes open when you do sitting meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 89

page_content: Be naturally attuned to your internal landscape without bringing your attention to your eyes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 89

page_content: While this is happening, attention will naturally stray to the process of seeing and you will know that seeing is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 89

page_content: It can be those moments when the awareness naturally settles on seeing and it can hit us with clarity that seeing is happening right now and being known.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 91

page_content: When we do not understand the reality of seeing, then sometimes putting attention on our eyes gets us lost in the things we see instead of recognizing that seeing is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 91

page_content: Sight or seeing is such an obvious object that we do not recognize it as such, simply because we don’t yet understand its mechanisms.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 91

page_content: It’s a similar story with the act of looking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 91

page_content: We want to pay attention to the mind that is doing the work of focusing on seeing in order to pick up something visually or to use certain information in order to function.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 91

page_content: You can’t physically know looking but you can know that the act of looking is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 91

page_content: It’s not something tangible but you can know that it is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 91

page_content: This is because reality is not solid.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 91

page_content: We can understand reality or we can know reality but that’s all we can do.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 7 on page 91

page_content: If we are able to be aware of awareness, able to know the mind that is doing the work of being aware, then that awareness will pick up seeing or looking on its own.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: SEEING IS DIFFERENT FROM LOOKING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 8 on page 91

page_content: These little skills are necessary, particularly in daily life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 91

page_content: All six sense doors are working while you’re eating.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 91

page_content: Do you know everything that is happening?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 91

page_content: If you forget yourself while you are eating, there will be a lot of eagerness there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 92

page_content: Eating often has a kind of excitement and eagerness that accompanies it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 92

page_content: So before you start eating, check and see if there is eagerness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 92

page_content: When this excitement calms down, awareness will become steadier.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 92

page_content: Taste arises on the tongue.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 92

page_content: Where does the feeling that this food is good arise?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 92

page_content: In the mind!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 92

page_content: They’re wholly separate.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 92

page_content: Of course when we speak, we say, “This tastes good.” You’ll have one and the same durian but some people will like it while others can’t stand it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 92

page_content: Durians have this one taste but the idea that this durian is either “heavenly” or “disgusting” happens in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 92

page_content: There was a Singaporean yogi who did not like durians and he watched the disliking mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 8 on page 92

page_content: When the disliking passed away, he then tried the durian.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 9 on page 92

page_content: Now he likes durians!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 92

page_content: Taste has nothing to do with it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 92

page_content: Some of you probably were turned off by the smell of durians from the very beginning.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 92

page_content: The first whiff made such a negative impression on the mind that it labeled everything about the durian as negative, beginning with that initial association with the sense of smell.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 92

page_content: So, let’s relate this to your daily life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 92

page_content: When you are outside and you judge a certain kind of sound as distracting or bad, then perhaps you will go looking for a place where there are no sounds.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 92

page_content: Can you find any place without sound?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 93

page_content: You have to understand the nature of sound.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 93

page_content: You may consider a certain kind of sound noisy, distracting, or bad.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 93

page_content: You may then go in search of a place without sounds.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THE PROCESS OF EATING, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 93

page_content: But can you find any place that is completely silent?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 93

page_content: We need insight into the nature of the thinking mind because much of our suffering comes from thoughts.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 93

page_content: Have you ever thought, “What if this car gets into an accident?” How did that feel?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 93

page_content: There is fear when “I” is involved but the mind is free when it knows a thought as just a thought.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 93

page_content: Thinking in the right way is part of the practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 93

page_content: Yogis are often afraid of thinking about meditation but in fact, to do any kind of work, we need to consider the situation, reflect, and think.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 93

page_content: We use wisdom to make fewer mistakes and we actively reflect on meditation work that’s productive and useful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 93

page_content: This helps us recognize what’s helpful in our practice and strengthen that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 93

page_content: What we are observing, experiencing and thinking about how to practice, it all comes together.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 93

page_content: The thinking mind in itself is neither wholesome nor unwholesome.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 93

page_content: It is the motivation of defilement or wisdom behind the thinking mind that determines the quality of the thought.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 93

page_content: We are concerned with this quality of mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 94

page_content: Naturally arising thoughts are not a problem because they are just objects to be known.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 94

page_content: Of course, if there is a defilement, we ought to deal with it and not let it carry on unchecked.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 94

page_content: If, on the other hand, it is wholesome, we can encourage it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 94

page_content: It is best not to observe the thinking mind alone.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 94

page_content: Also watch the feelings that accompany this thinking so that you can know when it is becoming too much.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 94

page_content: You can also gently allow the overthinking to continue and learn from it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 94

page_content: You will experience when it has become too much and having this experience will allow you to learn from overthinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 94

page_content: When there are strong wholesome qualities in the mind, it is difficult for the mind to suddenly change and become unwholesome; when there is a strong unwholesome train of thought going on, it’s difficult for it to suddenly switch and become wholesome.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 94

page_content: I experimented for myself when my mind was wholesome.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 94

page_content: I tried to intentionally think negative thoughts and realized that I could not.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 94

page_content: Know that not all Dhamma-related thinking will necessarily be wholesome.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 94

page_content: Sometimes a self-righteousness or attachment will be fueling it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 94

page_content: When Dhamma-related thinking has to do with your actual practice, it’s probably helpful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 94

page_content: It’s helpful to think about the Dhamma and it’s okay if you don’t understand things straight away.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 95

page_content: The Buddha recommended this kind of consideration because you never know when the mind might be in the right state and suddenly understand!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 95

page_content: Take in enough information to help you understand whether this information is beneficial, suitable, too much, too little, or balanced.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 95

page_content: If you can see the wanting to think, know that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 95

page_content: Observe the intensity of the wanting.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 95

page_content: When, through skillful observation, the wanting diminishes, the thoughts will also diminish.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: THINKING PROCESSES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 95

page_content: If you can’t see the intentions to think just switch back and forth between thoughts and body as objects.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 95

page_content: Do you know that you are walking when you are walking around in daily life?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 95

page_content: You will often be lost in thought.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 95

page_content: If you realize that this is happening, just know, “The mind is thinking.” What is important is what is happening in this moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 95

page_content: You can do what you need to do when you arrive at your destination; there’s no need to think about it now.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 95

page_content: This way the mind will also think less about the future.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 95

page_content: You can expend a lot of mental energy speculating about the future without actually knowing what is going to happen.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 95

page_content: If you discover a very different outcome than what you had expected, you have spent a lot of energy on imagination!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 97

page_content: There was a businessman who went to the market to buy goods wholesale to resell later.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 97

page_content: His journey to the market was filled with thoughts of various prices, how he would try to get there before everybody to get the first pick of goods.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 97

page_content: When he arrived he found there was nothing there for him!
