metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 97

page_content: This businessman was a yogi and so he was aware of what his mind was doing this whole time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: LOST IN THOUGHT, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 97

page_content: He realized how much time he had wasted speculating and determined that on future market trips he would relax and make decisions when he arrived.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 97

page_content: The concept or story is about the past or future but the knowing of that is in the present moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 97

page_content: You need to consider how you might respond to a certain situation so that you arrive prepared, but this is not the same as worrying about a situation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 97

page_content: If you are planning and knowing with wisdom that this is happening, that’s the present moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 97

page_content: Planning is necessary but could be done with either defilements or wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 97

page_content: Do you worry when you’re planning?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 97

page_content: Some people plan with greed and others with anxiety but there is a way to plan and think in a relaxed way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 97

page_content: Thinking about past events will also happen naturally from time to time and there’s nothing you can do about it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 97

page_content: You can’t go back and change the situation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 98

page_content: You can only revisit the past in thought.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 98

page_content: However, you can learn a lesson and not repeat the same mistakes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: MEMORIES AND PLANNING, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 98

page_content: One yogi realized that the idea of “one second” of time was a concept and the past dropped away for him with this understanding.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 98

page_content: Meditating at work is a skill and by skill, I mean lots of practice!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 98

page_content: Initially, of course, when you pay attention to outside phenomena, you can’t concentrate on phenomena inside; when you put your mind inside you can’t really be aware of what is happening outside.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 98

page_content: Initially you might not be able to be aware of inside and outside when you are working but try to practice whenever you can to allow momentum to build.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 98

page_content: At some point awareness will kick in naturally while you’re doing something you’re really absorbed in.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 98

page_content: Getting to this level of awareness requires consistent practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 98

page_content: When you need to be working, just do it fully.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 98

page_content: Sometimes you will have more time to devote to your practice and do it freely and you will know something like, “Oh the mind is thinking about this so it can do that.” When you build the habit of noticing the mind at work then you will notice that awareness just starts popping up because it becomes a habit for the mind to recognize itself doing work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 98

page_content: This will just come in naturally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 98

page_content: Allow this to happen.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: A yogi once asked a question about building up continuous practice, “How much should we do?” I said, “Do it 50-50.” The yogi was a psychiatrist so he thought a moment about my suggestion and asked, “How do you measure?” (Laughter.)

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: I considered it and realized that it wasn’t as much about a division of 50-50 as much as allowing the mind to do its work naturally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 99

page_content: If it is very practiced then the knowing happens naturally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: When you have a continuous practice that reminds and remembers and knows the mind at work, then it gets to the point where it becomes effortless because the mind becomes so familiar and so intimate with itself.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: It is always with itself and it likes being with itself.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 99

page_content: That’s when it allows the mind to do anything because it doesn’t mind; it’s always with itself.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 99

page_content: The operative word is continuously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 99

page_content: Although it is difficult in the beginning, any amount of effort you put in brings momentum and that in turn makes it more effortless and continuous in the future.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 99

page_content: That’s why the Buddha said to practice continuously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 4, sentence number 7 on page 99

page_content: WHEN THE MIND SAYS, “IT’S NOT FAIR.” When something happens, the mind starts making judgments, sets up parameters, and develops ideas of what’s appropriate and boundaries of “you” and “me.” Once hooked on these ideas, the mind won’t like it when one of these ideas is violated.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 100

page_content: We encounter this all the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 100

page_content: In Western society, people wait in a queue or line because there is a belief that people should wait their turn for something.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 100

page_content: There is a dissonance when someone cuts that queue/line and thoughts that they should be in line start coming up.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 100

page_content: Or different thoughts of fairness might come up.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 100

page_content: There could be another kind of belief, “I wanted to get to my food and she has slowed that down by jumping the queue.” The mind has convinced itself of all these strands of thought!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: AWARENESS AT WORK, paragraph number 5, sentence number 6 on page 100

page_content: At times like these, we should ask ourselves, “What internal belief is being thwarted here?” Right now, someone’s actions are frustrating these beliefs and the mind justifies this anger against the other person!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: STRINGING EACH OTHER ALONG, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 100

page_content: In daily life, we give each other compliments and thank each other for their compliments.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: STRINGING EACH OTHER ALONG, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 100

page_content: We string each other along by reinforcing the need to look good and feel good.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: STRINGING EACH OTHER ALONG, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 100

page_content: If instead we had said, “You look really terrible,” the listener might get irritated because people want to feel good.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: STRINGING EACH OTHER ALONG, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 100

page_content: We are often at the mercy of other people’s words, compliments or insults and are generally automatically affected.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: STRINGING EACH OTHER ALONG, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 101

page_content: When there is an understanding of how these thoughts work in the background, the wanting will disappear.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 101

page_content: It is very important that there is a right attitude when we are working in the world.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 101

page_content: Sometimes we may feel like we are being taken advantage of or being taken for granted.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 101

page_content: How different would it be if we thought these people were acting in a certain way because they didn’t know any better?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 101

page_content: If someone were bullying you, it would be really tough to handle!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 101

page_content: You may get tense, agitated or angry with the bully.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 101

page_content: What if you thought that this person was only inadvertently doing these things because she didn’t know better?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 101

page_content: How would you feel then?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 101

page_content: You can better understand and forgive.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 9 on page 101

page_content: SELF-JUDGMENTS “Bad” is just a label.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 10 on page 101

page_content: Don’t label yourself that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 11 on page 101

page_content: When the mind is wholesome, the person is good and when the mind is unwholesome, the person is bad.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 12 on page 101

page_content: It’s only for that moment and that’s always changing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 1, sentence number 13 on page 101

page_content: Continue to practice and take your time; the fact that you are practicing shows that your mind wants to become better and that means that the mind will become better.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: When doubt is strong in the mind, do not pay attention to these thoughts.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 103

page_content: Once we give attention to these kinds of thoughts, they suck us in and grow.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 103

page_content: We can anchor ourselves to feelings instead and not give any power to the conceptual thinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 103

page_content: Follow the same principle for other strong, unwholesome states.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 103

page_content: When we look at the feeling and discontinue looking at the thoughts, it will help the mind calm down.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 103

page_content: Then we can look at these thoughts and feelings together when we feel we’re ready.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: Everyone has his or her own path and there’s a natural course that needs to unfold for each person.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 103

page_content: You can watch what’s happening in the mind and understand it, but you can’t force it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 103

page_content: You may learn a technique at a retreat, but it is when you go home that you can apply that technique all the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: FEELING LIKE BEING TAKEN ADVANTAGE OF, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 103

page_content: That’s when your life changes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PRECONCEPTIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: How do you experience silence?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PRECONCEPTIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 103

page_content: How do you experience the stillness of a garden or the woods?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PRECONCEPTIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 103

page_content: I’ve asked different people this question.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PRECONCEPTIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 103

page_content: Fear comes up for some people.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PRECONCEPTIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 103

page_content: Youngsters tell me that they get bored when there’s nothing to occupy them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PRECONCEPTIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 103

page_content: As you can see, good or bad depends on the person experiencing it, depending on their preconceived feelings of it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PRECONCEPTIONS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 104

page_content: There are tons of these accumulations that you have from childhood and you really want to see these ideas that have built up over the years.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PRECONCEPTIONS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 104

page_content: If these little preconceptions are seen properly, then the mind will no longer be disturbed by what is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: PRECONCEPTIONS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 104

page_content: If you continue to meditate regularly, there ought to be fewer and fewer attachments.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 104

page_content: The market where I had my shop was a very busy place.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 104

page_content: Lots of shops were lined up side by side with narrow alleys between them.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 104

page_content: Shopkeepers sent goods in and out with carriers who ran back and forth for the shopkeepers.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 104

page_content: People would run quite blindly, not caring who was in the way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 104

page_content: I would get irritated whenever I had to get out of the way, which was quite often!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 104

page_content: I knew that people would run into me if I didn’t move aside, but also became annoyed that I was the one who always had to be careful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 104

page_content: This was a daily occurrence.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 104

page_content: When I began to practice continuously, I became mindful of my irritation with this market situation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 104

page_content: After being mindful of it regularly for a long time, I actually began to see it as a good practice to give space to these people to prevent accidents and I saw moving aside as a practice of generosity.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 104

page_content: As I practiced day after day and mindfulness arose, good actions also followed.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 105

page_content: The aversion that accompanied the critical mind decreased.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GIVING COMES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 105

page_content: With aversion gone, I began to feel mettā for these people.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 105

page_content: In daily life, where wisdom may be weak, you want initially to pay attention to awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 105

page_content: The mind can recognize the situation happening while awareness naturally collects data in the background.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 105

page_content: As awareness picks up more data from the experience, and the picture becomes complete and wholesome minds grow stronger, you will eventually stop acting out of defilements.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 105

page_content: Let’s say you are in a social situation and you have accidentally said something harsh because there was too much momentum to speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 105

page_content: Let awareness continue to know in the background while you’re talking to others.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 106

page_content: At some point of seeing this whole process, awareness will have enough of a picture that wisdom can arise and resolve the situation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 106

page_content: It is inevitable to make some wrong turns in daily life but you have to learn how to profit from these experiences.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 106

page_content: Don’t allow defilements to run freely or your situation will deteriorate.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 106

page_content: Imagine the alternate result if only delusion were operating.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 106

page_content: Perhaps that situation would become exaggerated.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 106

page_content: We have to recognize things for what they are.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 106

page_content: If the mind has a bad habit, we have to recognize that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 106

page_content: There is awareness so you’re keenly aware of what goes through your mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 106

page_content: It takes time and everybody has different characteristics.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 106

page_content: We all have faults and we need to look at what is in our mind and know it until the mind realizes that it doesn’t want to be that way anymore.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 8 on page 106

page_content: That realization will help you learn how to let go.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 106

page_content: We think that there are many people that we interact with in our daily lives.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 106

page_content: In fact, most of us will usually interact with a certain limited set of people over and over again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 106

page_content: How do we work with this?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 106

page_content: How can we strategize so that we become skillful?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 106

page_content: So long as there is the intention to have the Dhamma in our lives, then it will become easier.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 106

page_content: Having these experiences is very important and each experience gives you a life lesson.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 106

page_content: It’s like playing videogames, which I like to talk about with children.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 106

page_content: You work at it and reach the first level.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 106

page_content: You graduate to higher and higher levels and eventually win the prize.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 107

page_content: You know how to do this because you’ve got that experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 107

page_content: You know how you can win this game at this level.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 107

page_content: Of course if you don’t know how to learn from your experiences, you won’t get anything no matter how many life lessons come your way!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: We all have faults

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: and we need to look at what is in

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: our mind and know it

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: until the mind realizes that it

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: doesn’t want to be that way

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: anymore.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: That realization will help you

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: learn how to let go.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 108

page_content: We are not patient with gentle awareness; we do not believe it will work or trust that

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: DIFFERENT EXPERIENCES, DIFFERENT REACTIONS, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 108

page_content: it will grow into something.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 109

page_content: When I began to meditate in earnest at home, my awareness was inconsistent.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 109

page_content: I over-compensated for this spotty awareness by putting in a lot more personal effort.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 109

page_content: I wanted to be as mindful as possible but I was just too tired to do any more than that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 109

page_content: Even then, there was only so much I could do.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 109

page_content: After some time, I began to notice a lighter, general, but discontinuous awareness that, done day after day, formed a pattern of long-term practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 109

page_content: The momentum that came out of general awareness created these dots that came together to form a tsunami of awareness that could be seen clearly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 109

page_content: We do not believe gentle awareness will work or trust that it will grow into something so we are not patient with gentle awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 109

page_content: Personal effort uses our own effort while waiting patiently for momentum to grow uses a natural process, a natural law, and natural energy.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 109

page_content: Unfortunately, personal effort is short-lived and does not allow us to function in the day-to-day.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 109

page_content: We then begin to doubt our ability to live our lives with awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: The idea of figuratively looking lightly out of the corner of your eye describes a kind of awareness that watches the workings of the mind in its receiving state.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 110

page_content: The mind becomes aware of all the objects that match this mental frequency.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 110

page_content: Alternatively, when we turn and pay attention solely on the objects, and make too much effort to see these objects, it will feel like the objects have disappeared because the observer and observed are no longer at the same frequency.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 110

page_content: We can use an analogy with the optometrist here.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 110

page_content: When you go to the optometrist, they might have you try out different power lenses to determine what would work for you.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 110

page_content: When the power is right for you, then you see the letters clearly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 110

page_content: When the optometrist tries out a power that is too low or high, the letters on the chart become blurry.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 3, sentence number 8 on page 110

page_content: It’s the same deal with the mind that is at the right frequency with objects.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 3, sentence number 9 on page 110

page_content: While you may assume that putting in more personal effort will yield better results, you may still not be able to see clearly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: A light, general awareness allows us to continue functioning, to do the activities we need to do while a light recognition of what is happening works steadily in the background.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 110

page_content: This very gentle awareness, while not continuous in the beginning, will eventually gain momentum.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 110

page_content: When it becomes a natural, continuous awareness, it will really feel like you’re aware and you’ll feel like you’re living this awareness in everything that you’re doing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A Lighter Approach, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 111

page_content: For me, it was at this point that I really understood the true nature of vipassanā meditation and began to believe that it is possible to become enlightened while living life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 111

page_content: Meditators often forget to use their natural wisdom when they meditate at home because they haven’t practiced using it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 111

page_content: Investigate and use your wisdom to check in on whether awareness is present and how much effort is being used instead of blindly putting in more effort.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 111

page_content: Here are some considerations: Can you see your level of effort?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 111

page_content: Is there stability of mind?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 111

page_content: Do you know what that experience is like?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 111

page_content: Do you know your level of confidence or faith?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 111

page_content: Is wisdom at work?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 111

page_content: Meditators can normally recognize when there is awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 1, sentence number 9 on page 111

page_content: However, fewer meditators can recognize wisdom at work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 111

page_content: Wisdom is one word but it operates in so many ways.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 111

page_content: Sometimes we miss the fact that wisdom is there and doing its work because we aren’t familiar with the ways of wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 111

page_content: We are not close to wisdom like we are close with close friends.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 111

page_content: We’re more familiar with effort.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 111

page_content: I see meditators trying hard to stay awake.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 111

page_content: When we are sleepy we should instead do a bit of investigation of this sleepiness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 111

page_content: Why is there sleepiness now?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 113

page_content: What has brought it on?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 113

page_content: What does it feel like?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 113

page_content: Instead meditators react negatively to sleepiness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 113

page_content: They will try different tactics to fix the sleepiness instead of seeing it as it is and learning more about it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 6 on page 113

page_content: I see this playing out in the dhamma hall during sitting sessions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 7 on page 113

page_content: Some meditator starts falling asleep and the expression on his face shows that he is unhappy with himself as he picks himself up.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 8 on page 113

page_content: Blindly continuing these actions could just form bad habits.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: If sitting meditation makes you sleepy, sometimes being active can be good.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 113

page_content: If sitting meditation makes you sleepy, sometimes being active can be good.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 113

page_content: If you give the mind work to do, if it is being actively aware, then it will be alert and awake because it is doing something.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 113

page_content: You can also try sitting with your eyes open.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 113

page_content: We need to use wisdom the way we use our intelligence to solve problems, grow our businesses or think through what needs to be done at home and at work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 113

page_content: In daily life, we figure out how to do things, consult with the right people and strategize to make a profit.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 113

page_content: We need to recognize the presence of wisdom and apply similar skills to meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 113

page_content: PROBLEM-SOLVING FOR YOURSELF I often circulate around the Dhamma Hall of the Shwe Oo Min Meditation Center in the afternoons.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 6, sentence number 5 on page 113

page_content: There was a nun who sat right up front and I would see that her face would turn pink, over and over again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 114

page_content: Later in the interview I asked her why she was blushing repeatedly while she sat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 114

page_content: She said that it was because of her body ’s tendency to pass a lot of wind and she felt bad for the person sitting behind her, every time!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 114

page_content: So she went red in the face when her body did it again.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 114

page_content: I asked, “So what are you doing sitting in the front of the hall?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 6 on page 114

page_content: If you are concerned and if this is what your body does, sit at the back of the hall!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 7 on page 114

page_content: The door is wide open and you don ’t have to sit in front.” This meditator felt that she should have been sitting up front because she was a nun.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 8 on page 114

page_content: We cause ourselves trouble with our beliefs.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 9 on page 114

page_content: Suppressing such bodily needs can be uncomfortable and eventually make us sick.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 10 on page 114

page_content: Instead, we can find alternative solutions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 114

page_content: Set yourself free!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: USING WISDOM, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 114

page_content: Do what you need to do to meditate instead of holding certain ideas in your heads without considering an appropriate solution.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 114

page_content: Before I began meditating, I was the black sheep of the family.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 114

page_content: My words carried little to no weight because I was known to lie about things.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 114

page_content: But about three years into my practice, I spoke less and less, as I watched my speech very carefully.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 115

page_content: By then, everyone would listen whenever I spoke because what I said was something of value.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 115

page_content: They listened because they knew that what I said had a lot of thought behind it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 115

page_content: Sometimes my family would be unnerved because they knew what I was saying to be the truth.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 115

page_content: My family had regular Sunday social get-togethers where we would eat and then talk.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 115

page_content: The conversations usually revolved around the latest cars, movie stars, and such.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 115

page_content: I had been meditating for some time by then and found this to be a waste of time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 115

page_content: I considered what could be done and slowly an idea formed in my mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 115

page_content: One day I suggested that we all meditate together.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 115

page_content: All of us had meditation experience because my father used to bring us all to the monastery.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 115

page_content: So they agreed to meditate.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 2, sentence number 8 on page 115

page_content: We all sat for 15 minutes initially and then we would eat.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 115

page_content: After a while the idea crystallized, and we sat together in meditation every time we met.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 115

page_content: After eating or during the meal I then suggested a discussion and we would talk about life, whether we were happy or unhappy.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 115

page_content: I would ask them one by one, “How do you feel about your life?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 115

page_content: Do you feel satisfied with your life?” The party would turn serious; one brother replied that he was not satisfied with his life and another replied that it was 50:50.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 115

page_content: Slowly my siblings began to share the details of their lives and feelings, and someone would cry after sharing and get some relief.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 116

page_content: It was not something they had considered.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 116

page_content: Everyone was just sort of going through life, time was passing by, and they’d never given any thought to how they were living their own lives.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 116

page_content: From one week to the next, the questions would continue: If you’re not happy with your life, how do you make yourself happy?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 116

page_content: How do you deal with it?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 116

page_content: We brought the Dhamma into the conversation and it turned into Dhamma discussions.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: CREATING A DHAMMA COMMUNITY, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 116

page_content: With wisdom, you will also know how to help others.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 117

page_content: Everyone was just sort of

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 117

page_content: going through life,

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 117

page_content: time was passing by,

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 117

page_content: and they’d never given

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 117

page_content: any thought to how

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 117

page_content: they were living their own lives.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 118

page_content: I want yogis to know that mindfulness is a

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 118

page_content: lifetime commitment, not part-time work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 118

page_content: It is something that we really need to do all the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 118

page_content: Only when we see the nature of the mind

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: | | , paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 118

page_content: will we understand the nature of dhamma.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 119

page_content: yogi in one of my retreats had some questions surrounding free will.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 119

page_content: When we talk about the process of conditioning, remember there’s a stream of minds in the conditioned process.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 119

page_content: The conditions in one mind bring the effect into the next mind and then, whatever the mind chooses to do in the next moment adds a little to that quality, or takes away from that quality.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 119

page_content: It then passes away and then this condition gives rise to the next mind and then it passes away and the effect is given to the next mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 119

page_content: Every mind begins with something, a condition, but that mind has a moment in the present to choose its contribution to the moment in terms of action or non-action, before that mind passes away.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 119

page_content: “Everything is conditioned” just means that there is a stream that’s going on and the present moment has been conditioned by the past.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 119

page_content: At the same time, there is an element of choice in the present through wisdom because wisdom has free will.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 119

page_content: As a result, that wisdom has the ability to choose to take whatever action or non-action it would like.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: All things are conditioned but also you cannot imagine the vastness of kamma that you have accumulated that has the potential to flower in every moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 120

page_content: If somebody has developed a lifetime habit of anger, the person will become angry with a little trigger.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: If the person has studied meditation, starts practicing and understands the benefits of meditation in the present moment, this person may gradually develop to the point where, with awareness + wisdom, the person may possibly be able to consciously decide what to do.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 120

page_content: This person could decide whether to continue to be angry, on the one hand, or try to be aware of the anger and grow in wisdom as a result, on the other.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 120

page_content: This choice constitutes free will.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 120

page_content: If only delusion were present, then the conditioning is set and this person would act entirely according to that conditioning.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: When this kind of mind is not interested in wisdom, the mind is just a push-button system that is triggered by external forces.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 120

page_content: Conditioning will dictate all outcomes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 120

page_content: Awareness allows the mind to choose.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: You might hear someone talk about something or you might come across a book that opens up a bit of wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 120

page_content: You may borrow some wisdom from a book or person, consider it, and apply it to your life to bring about more wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 120

page_content: In this way, we can see how awareness + wisdom yields freedom of choice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 121

page_content: While I’ve said all this, I also don’t advise yogis to reflect too much on these matters.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 121

page_content: It’s easy to get lost in conceptualization and abstraction on these topics, and thus to drift far from the present moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: A       CAUSE AND EFFECT CHAIN , paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 121

page_content: One of the conditions for wisdom to arise is to think deeply about the Dhamma, but it absolutely needs to be grounded in Right View and Right Thought.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BUILDING UP INSIGHTS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 121

page_content: When we are experiencing an emotion, we can both be with the emotion and understand the nature of the mind that is feeling aversion or craving for this emotion.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BUILDING UP INSIGHTS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 121

page_content: This is a more powerful realization than just being relieved of the emotion for a moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BUILDING UP INSIGHTS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 121

page_content: Understanding that this is just mind, and there’s no one here, but only conditions is an insight.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BUILDING UP INSIGHTS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 121

page_content: Often, insight minds are momentary and this is normal.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BUILDING UP INSIGHTS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 121

page_content: We allow these insights to arise repeatedly and gather all these little insights many, many times.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BUILDING UP INSIGHTS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 121

page_content: The repeated arising of insight impresses that understanding into the mind and each mind that arises then passes along its qualities to the next mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BUILDING UP INSIGHTS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 121

page_content: When insights arise repeatedly, the inheritance of that insight for each successive mind becomes stronger.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BUILDING UP INSIGHTS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 121

page_content: That’s how we get lasting insights.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: BUILDING UP INSIGHTS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 122

page_content: If you are personally very involved in trying to do the practice, you will not be able to see what is going on naturally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 123

page_content: We always need to remember the difference between personal exertion and the energy of the dhamma taking over.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 123

page_content: If you are personally very involved in trying to do the practice, you will not be able to see what is going on naturally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 123

page_content: When you step back you are able to see that the process of awareness is already happening quite naturally.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 123

page_content: That’s why I sometimes ask yogis: Can you notice that you can hear even though you are not listening, that seeing is happening even though you are not trying to look at anything?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 123

page_content: Can you notice that even though you are not paying attention, your mind already knows things?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 123

page_content: I would like yogis, especially for those who have been practicing for years, to get to the point where they realize that without focusing or paying attention, that knowing is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 123

page_content: After many years of meditating, their practice must have gained momentum and they need to step back in order to see that this is happening.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 123

page_content: At that point, you need to switch from doing to recognizing.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 123

page_content: When the dhamma takes over, there is just a doing of what is necessary.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 123

page_content: Of course it is not possible to just switch, to immediately change the paradigm.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 123

page_content: But it is good to have this information because this will enable you to sometimes switch into this new mode.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 123

page_content: This way you will slowly understand what is actually happening and this will enable you to let go of the old paradigm.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 124

page_content: That’s why momentum is so important, when things continue under their own steam and you can really see that you are not involved.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 124

page_content: If you just practice continuously and correctly, understanding will arise.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: EFFORTLESS AWARENESS, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 124

page_content: Once you gain some understanding that this process is just happening, the mind will start seeing things more and more from this perspective.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 124

page_content: If you reflect on this life alone and add up the amount of time that you have spent trying to be aware, to grow the practice, and wisdom, you will find that it is a small amount of time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 124

page_content: Ignorance and defilements have been our companions from the time we were born.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 124

page_content: Things may seem impermanent but defilements always seem to be permanently there!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 124

page_content: If you understand cause and effect, however, you’ll see that defilements have always had their way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 124

page_content: They are the ones whose impressions have run very deep.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 124
