page_content: They are very strongly impressed in the mind and they leave a very long shadow to carry on their work through cause and effect.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 124

page_content: If our practice is intermittent, then we will not become any more skillful and any understanding will come to a standstill.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 124

page_content: If we neglect our practice altogether, delusion will begin to grow again and cloud over all the things that we once understood.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 125

page_content: So long as we continue meditating, we can maintain a certain level of understanding.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 125

page_content: Awareness is the ground that allows wisdom to arise over and over again and that’s why you have to be aware moment to moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 125

page_content: Any new understandings or wisdom passes away, just like everything else.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 125

page_content: The understanding of anicca (impermanence) and anatta (not-self, non- ego) are also impermanent.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 125

page_content: However, while that understanding may arise and also pass away, it will leave an impression for the next mind, which picks it up and in turn leaves an impression as it passes away.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 125

page_content: It is a zero sum game: You are either doing this or you are doing that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 125

page_content: If this is not present, then that is present.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 125

page_content: You are either moving forward or you are moving backward; there is no standing still.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 125

page_content: Wholesome minds will support each other just as unwholesome minds support each other.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 125

page_content: When something is burning and you throw in more water, the fire will go out.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 125

page_content: If flames are more intense, the fire will continue to burn.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 125

page_content: I want you to use your wisdom to continue the work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 125

page_content: You collect this gold dust a bit more each day, day by day.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 125

page_content: You learn from it all while this and that goes on in your lives.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 125

page_content: Mindfulness is a lifetime commitment, not a part-time job.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 125

page_content: It is something that you really, really need to do all the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 126

page_content: All the time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 126

page_content: Only when you see the nature of the mind will you understand the nature of dhamma.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 126

page_content: The mind is just arising and passing away, is so ephemeral, and the only input you can have to this process is the quality of awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 126

page_content: When awareness becomes natural, it means that you have been putting in the effort to keep it going: persistence is right effort.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 6 on page 126

page_content: Awareness starts staying.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 7 on page 126

page_content: Effort remains and it becomes much more natural and much more useful.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 126

page_content: Vipassanā wisdom cannot come about through intellectual thinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 126

page_content: It simply cannot be comprehended.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 126

page_content: Vipassanā insight is not something that can be conceptualized through images; it is a wholly new understanding and insight into principles, into nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 126

page_content: As we practice diligently and continuously, we will keep having these small understandings and insights over and over.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 126

page_content: Over the long haul, insights will become so persistent that they will work in tandem with awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 126

page_content: Once wisdom starts working together with awareness, our meditation moves to a higher level of understanding.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 126

page_content: We begin to have bigger insights.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 126

page_content: These bigger understandings have a life of their own; they have more power.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 126

page_content: They are not so dependent anymore on awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 126

page_content: Once we have had such insights, they will always be available and wisdom will always be there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 127

page_content: At this stage, awareness will step back to play a secondary role.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 127

page_content: It will always be present because wisdom cannot exist without awareness, but at this level of understanding, wisdom begins to have a life of its own.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page 127

page_content: Awareness will keep feeding wisdom and our understandings will grow in strength.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 8, sentence number 5 on page 127

page_content: At this stage, the mind always knows what to do, and it can happen that the practice becomes so easy that it will keep going even if we are not making any effort to practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 8, sentence number 6 on page 127

page_content: The more we practice, the more the mind understands how to practice and it doesn’t have to think about how to practice.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 8, sentence number 7 on page 127

page_content: It finds its way when wisdom starts to operate.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: The real benefit of the Dhamma is not to stop engaging with life issues but to approach the same tasks with wisdom instead of defilements, having understood how they work.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: May All Beings Be Happy!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 128

page_content: Everybody has wholesome and unwholesome qualities in the mind but learning to cultivate the wholesome qualities is meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: GATHERING MOMENTUM, COLLECTING GOLD DUST, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 128

page_content: How do we cultivate these good qualities?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: Satipaṭṭhāna, or meditation on the four foundations of mindfulness (body, feelings, mind and dhamma) cultivates the good qualities in our minds.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 129

page_content: Everybody has wholesome and unwholesome qualities in the mind but learning to cultivate the wholesome qualities is meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 129

page_content: How do we cultivate these good qualities?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 129

page_content: Meditation involves the things we observe (called objects) and that which is observing (the mind).

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 129

page_content: It is important to remember that sights, sounds, touch, taste, smells, and thoughts or the experiences arising at the six sense doors do not meditate.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 129

page_content: We do not have to do anything to these experiences because they will always be there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 129

page_content: These objects are just happening in the body and / or mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 1, sentence number 8 on page 129

page_content: Meditation is this latter part, the work of the mind, and the work to transform the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: A yogi has three jobs, and we will cover these in turn: 1) to have right view, 2) to be aware, and finally, 3) to practice continuously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 129

page_content: When we are experiencing something, we should remind ourselves that it is an object of meditation and that the mind is knowing it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 129

page_content: It should not be that this is a bad experience or this is a good experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 130

page_content: We would like to see things and regard things as they are.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 130

page_content: What is being known is just to be known and not to be loved or hated or desired or pushed away.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 130

page_content: Instead we remind ourselves that what is happening, what we’re experiencing, and what we’re observing is all nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 130

page_content: We should remind ourselves that objects will always follow their nature, that they are doing what they are doing, and that they are being seen, heard or experienced.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 130

page_content: If we don’t remind ourselves of this nature, the mind will vacillate, desiring in one moment and resisting in the next moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 130

page_content: Desire and resistance distort the “lenses” of the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 130

page_content: While the object itself is not important, we do need to know the object and to see the object clearly.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 130

page_content: Sometimes I say that we need a 60/40 awareness, which means we pay attention 60% to the observing mind and 40% to the object.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 130

page_content: We need to pay more attention to the observing mind because defilements like greed, anger, and delusion happen in the mind, and these defilements cover the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 130

page_content: They prevent us from seeing clearly; we cannot see objects for what they truly are.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 130

page_content: Let’s look more closely at the different pieces of meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 130

page_content: There are five qualities in our minds that do the work of meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 130

page_content: These qualities are awareness, perseverance, faith (or confidence), steadiness or stability of mind, and wisdom.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 131

page_content: These are the five qualities that we are trying to cultivate in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 131

page_content: There are also six sense objects arising at the six sense doors.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 131

page_content: These six sense objects are our stepping-stones in that we use the objects that arise at the six sense doors to cultivate the five good qualities of the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 131

page_content: When we meditate, any phenomenon or experience that is being known becomes an object.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 131

page_content: We also need to be consciously aware of what we know.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 131

page_content: We also need to know how to regard objects, to have the right view toward the objects, and be able to consider them in the right way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 131

page_content: We can do one of these things: we can remember that the objects are just nature, or that the objects are just things that are being known.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 131

page_content: In Satipaṭṭhāna, mindfulness or awareness (as I use the two words interchangeably) is the first of the five wholesome faculties of the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 131

page_content: We then cultivate the other four faculties through continuous mindfulness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 131

page_content: Simply, mindfulness is about remembering to be present in this moment, knowing any one of or all of the six sense objects (mind and/or body) that are happening right now.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 131

page_content: In English, I like to use words like awareness, mindfulness, remembering, not forgetting, recognizing, and noticing for the Pāḷi word of sati.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 6, sentence number 5 on page 131

page_content: I prefer not to use terms like concentrate or penetrate into the object because I don’t like the strong sense of effortful energy that is suggested by those words.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 132

page_content: I was once sitting in a car driven by a yogi.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 132

page_content: When another car passed by us the yogi asked, “Is it enough for me to know it as meditation awareness if I knew that a car passed by?” No.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 132

page_content: It’s not quite there yet.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 132

page_content: We would all know that a car had passed by whether we are meditating or not.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 132

page_content: How is meditation-knowing different from regular-knowing?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 7, sentence number 6 on page 132

page_content: There ’s seeing, hearing, and thinking and the meditator knows consciously that these things are being experienced.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 7, sentence number 7 on page 132

page_content: A non-meditator knows that a car has passed by but does not reference it to his own experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 7, sentence number 8 on page 132

page_content: He may instead refer just to the concept of what has happened where the thought has already processed the whole experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 7, sentence number 9 on page 132

page_content: That is why I say awareness is when you are aware of the six sense objects as your direct experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 132

page_content: For example, if I am sitting on a rug right now and I’m aware of this rug, this is not considered meditation awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 132

page_content: When I am aware of the process of seeing or feeling that allows me to see and feel and interpret this rug as a rug, this is my experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 132

page_content: I am aware of this experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page 132

page_content: Being aware of only the conceptual part of the experience is not sati.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 8, sentence number 5 on page 132

page_content: We are not as interested in the concept of a rug in-as-much as we are in the process of seeing or feeling.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 8, sentence number 6 on page 132

page_content: We need concepts in order to function in daily life, but in meditation we want to know the underlying process of what is happening in the mind, of nature, and of reality.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 133

page_content: So you are aware and you maintain that awareness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 133

page_content: You do not have to use much energy to be aware.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 133

page_content: There’s no need to focus hard but you do need to use right effort to keep reminding yourself to go continuously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 9, sentence number 4 on page 133

page_content: If you can use energy in that right way, you can go on practicing the whole day without feeling tired.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 9, sentence number 5 on page 133

page_content: Try to sustain that awareness for longer periods of time throughout the day.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 133

page_content: You can take one object that you know lightly and use that as a grounding object to explore more widely.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 133

page_content: When I was practicing continuously in daily life, I would use feeling as my touchstone.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 133

page_content: From there I began to notice how thoughts affected feelings and how feelings affected thoughts.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 10, sentence number 4 on page 133

page_content: I slowly learned and understood all these relationships.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 133

page_content: You can sit, walk, and lie down as you meditate in your daily lives.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 133

page_content: The posture is not important, and is even less so in daily life where you have to go about your business doing things.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 133

page_content: The timetable, however, is all the time from the moment you wake up in the morning until the moment you fall asleep at night.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 133

page_content: You also need to know how to view these objects that are arising.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 133

page_content: Whether they are sense objects or your experience, you need to view them as nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 12, sentence number 3 on page 133

page_content: Nature means it is impossible to find a “you” or a “me.” Nature is all just a process of cause and effect.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 12, sentence number 4 on page 133

page_content: There’s nothing inherently good or bad in these objects.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 134

page_content: If you are developing greed or aversion towards any kind of sound, it indicates that you have a wrong view of the experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: Come back to the right view when you face difficulties with certain objects: consider sound as just a sound.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 14, sentence number 2 on page 135

page_content: It is an object and it is just nature.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 14, sentence number 3 on page 135

page_content: This is the view that you should take.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 14, sentence number 4 on page 135

page_content: The instruction in the Satipaṭṭhāna Sutta is to recognize the experience as it is.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 14, sentence number 5 on page 135

page_content: If you are developing greed or aversion towards any kind of sound, it indicates that you have a wrong view of the experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 14, sentence number 6 on page 135

page_content: For someone with right view, samādhi, or stability of mind is automatically there.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: A concept is just a name given to reality.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 15, sentence number 2 on page 135

page_content: When you become aware of reality, you can see both the mind that is conceptualizing and reality.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 15, sentence number 3 on page 135

page_content: You will notice a sound as just a sound (reality) and also an interpretation of the particular sound (concept).

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 15, sentence number 4 on page 135

page_content: When you are crossing the street and you hear a car coming, then you also need to watch out to see if a car is coming.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 15, sentence number 5 on page 135

page_content: You can know that sound is just sound but wisdom must also understand what that sound means.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 15, sentence number 6 on page 135

page_content: There are two kinds of wisdom at work here: the wisdom that understands the concept of an approaching car and the wisdom that understands reality of a sound is a sound.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 16, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: Understanding is different from just experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 16, sentence number 2 on page 135

page_content: Understanding means you get it: This is reality.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 16, sentence number 3 on page 135

page_content: Sometimes experience does not mean that you truly understand it and the view changes.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 16, sentence number 4 on page 135

page_content: You can know that something is an object, which you can’t touch or feel but you just understand that this is something that is being known and therefore has the role of being an object.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 1 on page 136

page_content: The same instructions apply for the thinking mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 2 on page 136

page_content: When you notice that the mind is thinking, know it.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 3 on page 136

page_content: If there is less thinking, know that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 4 on page 136

page_content: If there is more thinking, know that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 5 on page 136

page_content: If there is no thinking, know that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 6 on page 136

page_content: Thinking is the mind and the definition of mind is that which thinks and that which knows.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 7 on page 136

page_content: Problems arise when there is aversion to thinking, when you have the idea that you should not be thinking when you are meditating.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 8 on page 136

page_content: All sorts of resistance follow that idea.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 9 on page 136

page_content: There is no need to struggle with your thoughts because the thinking mind is never a problem.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 10 on page 136

page_content: If there is a lot of thinking going on, there is no need to reduce it!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 11 on page 136

page_content: All you have to do is acknowledge over and over again that the mind is thinking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 17, sentence number 12 on page 136

page_content: After all, you are only able to recognize that amount of thinking because there is awareness!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 18, sentence number 1 on page 136

page_content: Yogis label the times when they are lost in thought and don’t know that they’re thinking because they are lost in thoughts as a wandering mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 18, sentence number 2 on page 136

page_content: The mind arises in the present moment and passes away in the present moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 18, sentence number 3 on page 136

page_content: Thoughts about home or work arise in the mind and we can acknowledge these thoughts arising each time it happens.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 18, sentence number 4 on page 136

page_content: Actually the mind does not wander anywhere nor does it go anywhere; it thinks about the past and future in the present moment.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 1 on page 137

page_content: You can relate to pain in the same way.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 2 on page 137

page_content: When you notice even a little bit of pain, first check your attitude.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 3 on page 137

page_content: If the mind is feeling uncomfortable, adjust the attitude, remind yourself that this is nature and then, having brought in the right view, watch the discomfort or the aversion in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 4 on page 137

page_content: First, watch the resistance and then you can watch the actual physical sensations.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 5 on page 137

page_content: To begin with, why do you observe pain?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 6 on page 137

page_content: Do you want to make it go away?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 7 on page 137

page_content: It is natural for the body to become uncomfortable if we sit still for long periods.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 8 on page 137

page_content: We should observe pain because we want to understand something about the nature of pain; we don’t observe pain in hopes that it will go away.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 9 on page 137

page_content: Why do you think your leg hurts when it hurts?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 10 on page 137

page_content: The simplest answer is that your leg hurts because you have a leg.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 11 on page 137

page_content: Where there is a body, there will also be physical pain.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 19, sentence number 12 on page 137

page_content: That is awareness and right view that we can develop.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 20, sentence number 1 on page 137

page_content: The final, important piece of a yogi’s three jobs is to practice continuously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 20, sentence number 2 on page 137

page_content: Whatever you do, first be aware of yourself, and then go about your task.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 20, sentence number 3 on page 137

page_content: You may have fairly continuous mindfulness when you are sitting in meditation but you will eventually get up.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 20, sentence number 4 on page 137

page_content: What do you know first when you get up?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 20, sentence number 5 on page 137

page_content: It could be your hand or legs moving or just the energy you’re using to put strength into what you do.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 20, sentence number 6 on page 137

page_content: Know that and what’s happening after that.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 20, sentence number 7 on page 137

page_content: It’s even better when you know all your movements and if you know what is happening in the mind at the same time.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 21, sentence number 1 on page 138

page_content: When you do sitting meditation you can take the whole body as an object and know anything that becomes obvious and see it change.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 21, sentence number 2 on page 138

page_content: If you are used to using an anchor, then use that anchor as a starting point.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 21, sentence number 3 on page 138

page_content: You can use awareness of breathing in and out at the nostrils (ānāpānasati), rising and falling of the abdomen, or sounds as anchors.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 21, sentence number 4 on page 138

page_content: These are just a few examples.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 21, sentence number 5 on page 138

page_content: You can use any object that is easy for you to be aware of as an anchor to establish mindfulness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 21, sentence number 6 on page 138

page_content: The anchor just starts the mind off on being mindful and once you are aware of any object for some time, the mind will actually start to know other objects.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 21, sentence number 7 on page 138

page_content: That is good.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 21, sentence number 8 on page 138

page_content: It means that mindfulness is getting better.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 21, sentence number 9 on page 138

page_content: When the mindfulness is good, it begins to feel like it knows four or five objects simultaneously.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 22, sentence number 1 on page 138

page_content: You are observing everything to understand the nature of this mind- body experience.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 22, sentence number 2 on page 138

page_content: What can you understand about these experiences?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 22, sentence number 3 on page 138

page_content: You have been practicing for a long time, you have been watching the body, watching the mind, and you have been doing it for years.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 22, sentence number 4 on page 138

page_content: What more have you understood about this body and this mind and the interactions between body and mind?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 23, sentence number 1 on page 138

page_content: The goal of Satipaṭṭhāna practice is to understand deeply.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 23, sentence number 2 on page 138

page_content: When there is right understanding, when we have true realization into the nature of things, there will be calm in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 23, sentence number 3 on page 138

page_content: This calm state is a by-product of understanding or insight.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 23, sentence number 4 on page 138

page_content: The real goal of this meditation is to understand.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 24, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: How can we understand the nature of pain if we just stared at it and it disappeared?

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 24, sentence number 2 on page 139

page_content: Please be patient.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 24, sentence number 3 on page 139

page_content: When there is pain, watch the resistance in the mind and do this only for as long as it is bearable.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 24, sentence number 4 on page 139

page_content: When you change your posture, be aware of two possible motivations behind it: 1) greed to be more comfortable, or 2) understanding that if you continue to sit in that position, your aversion will grow.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 24, sentence number 5 on page 139

page_content: You don’t want to be growing aversion any more than you want to be growing greed.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 24, sentence number 6 on page 139

page_content: Understanding that the pain might become too much, you let go of the pain and make yourself more comfortable to prevent the growth of aversion.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 25, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: Finally, let’s discuss talking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 25, sentence number 2 on page 139

page_content: We should try to practice right speech and only say what is necessary and wholesome and for this we do need mindfulness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 25, sentence number 3 on page 139

page_content: Speaking is all around us in daily life.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 25, sentence number 4 on page 139

page_content: You do not want to cut off speech without awareness of what you need to do.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 25, sentence number 5 on page 139

page_content: Use some restraint and know, consider and understand before you speak.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 25, sentence number 6 on page 139

page_content: Then you will also know if there is a need to talk.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 25, sentence number 7 on page 139

page_content: If you wanted to say something and you refrained from speaking then continue to know and recognize that you are not speaking but see the thinking going on in the mind.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 26, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: There are many things that can happen while you are talking.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 26, sentence number 2 on page 139

page_content: If you are triggered when you are talking to someone, the fact that you are triggered but are still there, with the person, might trigger other emotions like embarrassment or agitation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 26, sentence number 3 on page 139

page_content: While the mind may still be agitated, at the very least, you can still be aware.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 2 on page 141

page_content: The purpose of mindfulness of speech is not to have a perfectly wise response, but to have a mindful conversation.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 3 on page 141

page_content: You may have lost your balance in that time but what you need to know is that mindfulness is present through it all.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 4 on page 141

page_content: If you are strongly attached to ideas, there will be triggers when someone else presents a conflicting idea.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 5 on page 141

page_content: It’s like North and South Korea being at war, separated by ideology.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 6 on page 141

page_content: You can learn some lessons from these conversations when there is mindfulness.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 7 on page 141

page_content: Whenever we say something wrong, we will suffer a little bit but if mindfulness stays with us, we will always learn.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 27, sentence number 1 on page 140

page_content: If you are strongly attached to ideas, there will be triggers when someone else presents a conflicting idea.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 1 on page 141

page_content: Be a little extra careful at home.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 2 on page 141

page_content: It is so much more interesting at home when mindfulness is always present, and especially when we bring mindfulness to daily life situations.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 3 on page 141

page_content: In your talking to others you learn to see others’ intentions and learn to speak in a way that helps you to manage yourself and manage the conversation as well.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Appendix: Mindfulness in Brief, paragraph number 28, sentence number 4 on page 141

page_content: We begin to know what we are going to say before we say it and eventually have time to choose the skillful path.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: This wonderful gift of Dhamma comes to you from the community of meditators and supporters for whom the Dhamma

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: has touched in unfathomable ways.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: May you also reap the benefits of three levels of wisdom

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: through this Dhamma dana—

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: in understanding the profound Dhamma,

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: in practicing rightly the Dhamma and

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: in reaping the fruit of Dhamma.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: And may Nibbāna be the absolute finale.

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: Sādhu!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 142

page_content: Sādhu!

metadata.source: Source document: COLLECTING GOLD DUST: Nurturing the Dhamma in Daily Living, Chapter: Dedication, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 142

page_content: Sādhu!
